issue_title: Open Project menu and directory tree browsing
issue_body:
I have few comments below regarding the File menu and the directory tree browser - don't know if they are easy to fix or not, but would improve the usability of your great software! 

The File menu has New Project option, but there is no obvious way to open an existing Project... The "Open directory" offers to convert non-Project folders to Project, and opens the Project ones, however right click the folder  on the directory tree offers only "New Directory" and "Delete" - "Create/Open Project" would be very logical here!.. Also the "New Directory" should be changed to "New Folder"?..

I understand that once a Project is created, it can be reopened from the history on the bottom of File menu. However I have URH folder tree on my OneDrive, which I'm accessing from several different computers - the project created on one PC will not appear on the URH history list of another PC. An easy way to just Open Project, preferably directly from the directory tree, would help a lot. BTW, just double clicking the folder on the directory tree, even the Project folder, should not open the Project - I'm just browsing, and right-click "Open Project" when I need...

Also, once you select a folders using "Open directory" (should it be renamed to "Open Folder", for consistency with "Convert Folder to Project" below?..), there is no way to browse up the tree, even if you opt not to convert the folder to project...

Is it also possible to add a horizontal scroll to the directory tree? In many cases it's only necessary to see the right-most part of the folder/file name, and expanding the directory tree is stealing the limited screen estate.

Thank you.

issue_title: response_string can lead to false positives if value is a string
issue_body:
The correct way to do the `response_strings` test is to provide a list of strings:

```yaml
response_strings:
    - foo
    - bar
```

If you forget and do a string, though, it can sometimes pass because the value is use as an iterable and strings iterate. In the following example of the response contains the letters `f` and `o` the test will pass:

```yaml
response_strings:
    foo
```

Stronger type checking is required.

issue_title: setuptools doesn't find libraries under /opt/local/include
issue_body:
On macOS, I installed mpfr using macports. It is installed at `/opt/local/include/mpfr.h`.

However, when building, I get this error

    /usr/bin/clang -Wno-unused-result -Wsign-compare -Wunreachable-code -fno-common -dynamic -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -pipe -Os -DWITHMPFR -DWITHMPC -I/usr/local/include -I/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/include/python3.5m -c src/gmpy2.c -o build/temp.macosx-10.12-x86_64-3.5/src/gmpy2.o
    In file included from src/gmpy2.c:426:
    src/gmpy.h:252:12: fatal error: 'mpfr.h' file not found
    #  include "mpfr.h"
               ^~~~~~~~
    1 error generated.
    error: command '/usr/bin/clang' failed with exit status 1

I think it is because `setup.py` doesn't actually look where it is supposed to.
The [docs](https://gmpy2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/intro.html#detailed-instructions) state 
"setup.py will automatically search the following directories for the required libraries: /opt/local, /opt, /usr/local, /usr, /sw", but it isn't true.

I am not an expert of setuptools, but solved the problem like this:

    extensions = [
        Extension('gmpy2.gmpy2',
                  sources=sources,
                  include_dirs=['./src', '/opt/local/include'],
                  libraries=_libs,
                  extra_compile_args=_comp_args,
                  extra_link_args=link_args,
                  )
    ]

Maybe you want to add all the include folders in include_dirs?

issue_title: Two tasks blocked on the same file descriptor -> crash
issue_body:
While studying `_trap_io` in kernel.py, I came to two conclusions:
* I'm not clever enough to understand the `task._last_io` logic used to debounce selector registrations
* The way kernel.py uses its selector is fundamentally broken :-(. selector registrations are keyed off of the file descriptor only, so if you have two tasks that are interested in the same file descriptor then their registrations collide and things blow up.

For example, on current curio this test crashes:

```python
def test_read_write_on_same_socket(kernel):
    async def main():
        s1, s2 = socketpair()
        t1 = await spawn(s1.recv(1000))
        # Large enough send to trigger blocking on write:
        N = 10000000
        t2 = await spawn(s1.sendall(b"x" * N))
        # Above is the actual test -- right now it triggers a crash.
        # Rest of this is just to clean up:
        # Let t1 finish:
        await s2.sendall(b"x")
        # Let t2 finish:
        n = 0
        while n < N:
            n += len(await s2.recv(N))
        await t1.join()
        await t2.join()

    kernel.run(main())
```

So I thought ok, I'll fix both of these at the same time by making a new class that will sit between the kernel and the selector, and take care of multiplexing multiple waiters onto the same file descriptor, and also do debouncing.

You can see the code here: https://github.com/njsmith/curio/commit/7ece22e29089acc7efb4427608a9927de008e79d

(NB that particular commit is dangling off of some other random cleanups, so you wouldn't want to merge it directly, but it can be cherrypicked or just looked at.)

Two problems though:

1) It's slower -- the echo server microbenchmark on my laptop drops from ~40k/s to ~24k/s. There's part of me that thinks this is a good thing if it encourages us to stop paying attention to this microbenchmark, but it is nonetheless a thing...

2) It doesn't actually work in general, because the debouncing logic is too aggressive. What happens is:

   1. Some task is interested in fd 4
   2. Then it stops being interested in fd 4
   3. We make a note that if no-one has become interested in fd 4 by the
      time the next loop rolls around, then we should drop it from the
      underlying selector.
   4. Then our task closes fd 4.
   5. Then it (or some other task) opens a new file, which gets assigned fd 4.
   6. Then it blocks on this new file.
   7. The debouncing logic sees that it's still interested in fd 4, and it "knows" the selector is still watching fd 4, so it doesn't adjust the selector.
   8. But, epoll is keyed off actual files, not file descriptor
      numbers. It knows that these two fd 4s are not the same. In fact,
      it already removed fd 4 from its watch set when the first file
      descriptor was closed. So we are screwed.

But, looking at this... actually the same basic problem could happen to the current debouncing logic. The reason it doesn't is that the new debouncing logic is keyed off of file descriptors, while the old debouncing logic is keyed off of file objects. But you kind of *have* to key off of file descriptors if you want to handle the general case of arbitrarily many tasks that are interested in the same file descriptor, because the underlying selector logic is keyed off of file descriptors -- if you try to register two separate interests in the same fd but using different fileobjs, then the selector will blow up.

So, maybe we should just give up on debouncing?

It looks like asyncio avoids some of these issues by imposing the rule that there can only be one reader and one writer per file descriptor at any given time: https://github.com/python/asyncio/blob/eb4c3ffdddc415330b434cefa9187360dc934ce0/asyncio/selector_events.py#L260-L273

In practice, I can't imagine any situations where it would make sense to have two tasks both blocked on the readability or both blocked on the writeability of the same fd, so I guess this wouldn't be a terribly onerous restriction. Not sure how much imposing it simplifies things, though.

issue_title: Handle SSH hanging
Feature Request: Fake Binary Content
issue_body:
Let's say I want to write unit tests for my web service that has a file upload feature.
It would be great if I can fake random binary content of popular binary file types, especially images and documents, like:

- *.jpg
- *.pdf
- *.doc/docx

I think image uploading is probably the most common type of uploaded binary content, so it may be a good place to start.

**Why would someone need this?**
This could be great mainly for testing file upload APIs for several reasons.
It's way easier if the unit tests can generate fake binary content, so they will not depend on actual fixed binary files (and syncing them between teammates, YUCK).

**Some use case examples:**
- A user uploads any format of an image (jpg, png, bmp, etc.) and the server needs to recognize the image type (by its binary structure, headers etc.) and store/convert/compress/whatever according to it.
- A user uploads a document in a popular format (.pdf, .docx, .pages) and the server needs to read the content for parsing/analysis/whatever.

issue_title: pyvera throwing exception error on dimmer state refresh
issue_body:
Not sure if this is only happening to me or to anyone else as well? 
Maybe not many people are using Vera and HomeAssistant?

Under the get_brightness def, line 577 of the __init__.py file, there is a reference to the **_level_** variable as an integer **_(int(level))_**. 

When refreshing the state of my dimmers from Vera, they return a % value (e.g. 20%) which causes an error in parsing the __init__py file as the 'level' variable is not an integer in that case but a string.

This generally happens every 2 or 3 days. Not sure why it occurs that infrequently.
I believe this has happened with all versions I've used so far - v0.39.x up to current v0.44.2 but I didn't notice earlier as I was always restarting Home Assistant while I was playing with it/setting things up. I've been using it for just over a month now.
I only really noticed this issue as I am graphing temperatures in grafana/InfluxDB and after this error, the temperature sensors in Vera stop updating in Home Assistant and therefore the graphs are blank.

I have implemented a workaround that forces the level variable to an integer if it is not already.
I am sure there are more elegant ways to get the same result?

I have added the following line to my local version today (line 577) above where the error normally occurs. This should strip the % character and change **_level_** to an integer if it is not already one.

**_level = int(level) if type(level) == int else float(level.strip('%'))_**

Full section (including extra line) is as follows (from line 565):

    def get_brightness(self, refresh=False):
        """Get dimmer brightness.

        Refresh data from Vera if refresh is True, otherwise use local cache.
        Refresh is only needed if you're not using subscriptions.
        Converts the Vera level property for dimmable lights from a percentage
        to the 0 - 255 scale used by HA.
        """
        if refresh:
            self.refresh()
        brightness = 0
        level = self.get_value('level')
        level = int(level) if type(level) == int else float(level.strip('%'))
        percent = 0 if level is None else int(level)
        if percent > 0:
            brightness = round(percent * 2.55)
        return int(brightness)


Will report back in 2 days if that hasn't resolved my issue.
If it does, can we get something similar added into the main branch?

Thanks,
Alan

issue_title: Properly handle the common case of tests/ without an __init__.py
issue_body:
It is very common to have:

    proj/
        module/
            __init__.py
            ...
        tests/
            (no __init__.py)
            ...

The main process discovers the tests fine, but when the worker receives the name of the module, it fails to load it when `tests/` doesn't have an `__init__.py`.

Fixing this may also fix other run-green-from-a-different-directory-can't-load-tests problems.

issue_title: Heimdal Kerberos GSSAPI bind SSF issue
issue_body:
Binding actually mostly works with Heimdal Kerberos, however it appears to negotiate a SSF (Security Strength Factor) value of 0 (given the below slapd logs with `sasl_ssf=0`) when I believe it should be negotiating a value of 56:

```
conn=1024 op=2 BIND dn="uid=jvperrin,ou=people,dc=ocf,dc=berkeley,dc=edu" mech=GSSAPI sasl_ssf=0 ssf=256
do_bind: SASL/GSSAPI bind: dn="uid=jvperrin,ou=people,dc=ocf,dc=berkeley,dc=edu" sasl_ssf=0
send_ldap_response: msgid=3 tag=97 err=0
```

Later on in the log, when a search is performed, since the SSF is 0 (instead of the required 56), the search errors because it is denied by the ACL, so this results in an empty list returned by ldap3 from the search:

```
<= check a_authz.sai_sasl_ssf: ACL 56 > OP 0
[...]
<= acl_mask: no more <who> clauses, returning =0 (stop)
=> slap_access_allowed: search access denied by =0
```

I couldn't tell whether this is an issue with `ldap3` or an issue with `gssapi` even after stepping through a lot of the connection and bind code to try to narrow down the issue. Let me know if more logs would be useful, but the rest of the logs appear to be extra spam that is also present in a successful request, so I didn't think it would help much. Using [python-ldap](https://github.com/python-ldap/python-ldap) works fine, but it doesn't yet have great Python 3 support and we'd already use `ldap3` quite a lot, so we'd like to migrate to use this library for queries where binding is required. The test script I'm using is [here](https://gist.github.com/jvperrin/866735be9421e0b4c7188af8024ddfef), it's essentially the [same script](https://gist.github.com/anonymous/60ba84fecd0492c28e67) used by @chriskuehl in #33 when he encountered the same problem with an empty list being returned from queries.

issue_title: formatters.format_ad_timestamp(): Local variable 'timestamp' referenced before assignment
issue_body:
As you can see [here](https://github.com/cannatag/ldap3/blob/master/ldap3/protocol/formatters/formatters.py#L107) the local variable `timestamp` is used in the exception handler, where it is not defined.

Running in the exception handler raises an `UnboundLocalError`. This happens for example if you want to pass a `datetime` object to the Active Directory.

issue_title: Ds4drv with Retropie stops keyboard in emulators
issue_body:
After adding the `/usr/local/bin/ds4drv --led 000008 &` line to the `/etc/rc.local` file, the controller would connect and be recognized by emulationstation and as long as the controller was connected, the keyboard would also work.  If the controller was not connected, the keyboard would navigate emulationstation but would not function in any of the emulators.  Almost like it was disconnecting the keyboard as a controller.

This complicates things if you want to be able to connect a wireless controller and also use an I-Pac or some other arcade controller interface that emulates a keyboard when not using the wireless controller.

issue_title: LIST pattern eats away and silently ignores anything between unrelated LIST_ITEM patterns (unless )
issue_body:
LIST pattern silently eats away and anything between nonsibling LIST_ITEM patterns. The only exception is if a comment exists between lists.

For the following input
```yaml
a:
  - 1
  - 2 # comment between a and b
b:
  - 3
  - 4
c:
  - 5
  - 6
# comment between c and d
d:
  - 7
  - 8
```
parse_string returns
```python3
{
    u'a': [
        1,
        2,
    ],
    u'b': [
        3,
        4,
        5,
        6,
    ],
    u'd': [
        7,
        8,
    ],
}
```
while the expected output is
```python3
{
    u'a': [
        1,
        2,
    ],
    u'b': [
        3,
        4,
    ],
    u'c': [
        5,
        6,
    ],
    u'd': [
        7,
        8,
    ],
}
```

issue_body:
Hi Mike -- I produced a 1-2-3 scaled cuboid in Blender, then triangularized it in MeshMixer. I will paste the raw Blender model and the triangularized MeshMixer model in OBJ format below, but the rest of this issue concerns only the MeshMixer model (trimesh found the original Blender model to be not watertight, so I stopped analyzing it).

issue_title: request fetch <user>/<repo> must be same as the default gitlab remote
issue_body:
git repo says it supports `user>/<repo>` for the `request fetch` command , like the `request list` command, but it seems to always look for a `gitlab` remote, and the `<request>` id must be on the `gitlab` remote, which means the use of `<user>/<repo>` on the command line is superfluous.

```
git-repo [--path=<path>] [-v...] <target> request <user>/<repo> (list|ls)
git-repo [--path=<path>] [-v...] <target> request <user>/<repo> fetch <request> [-f]
```

```
$ git lab request coala/package_manager list
List of open requests to merge:
 id	title                                                       	URL
 31	MavenRequirement: Prevent test fails                        	https://gitlab.com/coala/dependency_management/merge_requests/31
 30	DistributionRequirementTest: Fix assertRaisesRegex          	https://gitlab.com/coala/dependency_management/merge_requests/30

$ git lab request coala/package_manager fetch 31
Fatal error: Could not find remote gitlab
```

I have a remote for `coala/package_manager` , but it isnt called `gitlab`. (it is my `origin`)

If I do name that remote `gitlab`, then I dont need to specify `coala/package_manager` on the command line, as it is the default anyway, so I can simply use `request fetch 31`.

Rather than removing this unusable feature, I would prefer that it works, at least when there is already a remote for the requested `<user>/<repo>`, but it should also be relatively easy to fetch the MR without a remote for the requested `<user>/<repo>`.

issue_title: Absolute focus nodes don't work when testing against local shexTest image
issue_body:
When evaluating RDF in the form:
```
<x> :p1 "p1-0" .
```
`<x>` resolves to "file:///usr/path/x" if this is loaded from "/usr/path/foo.ttl".  The focus, sht:focus <x> in the manifest resolves to the absolute manifest location (https://raw.githubusercotent.com/shexSpec/shexTest/master/validation/x).  We need to tweak the focus node in `manifest_tester.py` to match

issue_title: Redesign flatsym module
vcrpy replays recently added requests
issue_body:
After storing a request in the VCR, sometimes vcrpy will replay that request later, even though every request should only be used once.

I made a [little script](https://gist.github.com/Sjord/06bede5fb2702b360413) to reproduce this problem. It starts with an existing YAML file so that `Cassette._rewound` is True. Then when you request the same URL two times, the first request is made to the server and stored, and the second request is replayed. The output looks like this:

```
29e619d8
29e619d8
f59ea0cc
f59ea0cc
da52bd77
da52bd77
760af8e3
760af8e3
34c7af33
34c7af33
```

I would expect every request to hit the server and be stored in the YAML file, and none be replayed. Maybe `Cassette.append` should set the `play_counts` to 1?

issue_title: Wrong package versions within virtualenv
issue_body:
I'm not sure how widespread this issue is, or if it's something particular to my setup, but when I use watermark to report package information (using -p) within a jupyter notebook that is running in a virtualenv, version information about system level packages are reported rather than packages installed within my environment. If a package is installed in my virtualenv and not installed at the system level, then I end up getting `DistributionNotFound` exceptions when I try to report the version number using watermark.

The problem stems from the call to `pkg_resources.get_distribution`. If I use `get_distribution` directly from within my notebook to report the version of e.g. `numpy` it shows my system level numpy info (v 1.11.0) rather than info about numpy installed within my virtualenv (v 1.11.1). For example:

> `pkg_resources.get_distribution('numpy')`
> `numpy 1.11.0 (/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages)`
 
Similarly

> `%watermark -p numpy`
> `numpy 1.11.0`

But when I check the version of what is imported:

> `import numpy as np`
> `np.__version__`
> `'1.11.1'`

If I use `pkg_resources` from a python console or script running within a virtualenv, it reports the proper information about packages. But if I use it from an ipython console or jupyter notebook it reports system level information.

If this is a widespread issue, should `watermark` find another more robust solution to getting package information other than using `pkg_resources`?

issue_title: Support optional GraphQL arguments for QueryValidator
issue_body:
Long story short: GraphQL arguments can be optional without default value.

----

From GraphQL [documentation](http://graphql.org/learn/schema/#arguments),

> Arguments can be either required or optional. When an argument is optional, we can define a default value - if the unit argument is not passed, it will be set to METER by default.

From GraphQL [specification](https://facebook.github.io/graphql/#sec-Required-Non-Null-Arguments),

> Arguments can be required. If the argument type is non‐null the argument is required and furthermore the explicit value null may not be provided. Otherwise, the argument is optional.

----

But in Hiku there is no way to provide optional arguments, when using `QueryValidator` [cause of](https://github.com/vmagamedov/hiku/blob/0ebd453c6af9f5bf8de28353858a149f5eb5db22/hiku/validate/query.py#L113),

```py
    def visit_option(self, obj):
        default = _undefined if obj.default is None else obj.default
        value = self._options.get(obj.name, default)
        if value is _undefined:
            node, field = self.for_
            self.errors.report('Required option "{}.{}:{}" is not specified'
                               .format(node, field, obj.name))
```

Hiku enforces all options to be required or use non-null default values, which not conforms GraphQL specification cited above. As result right now there is impossible to mix `QueryValidator` and optional arguments.

----

Code to reproduce,

```py
    Field(
        'count_documents',
        Integer,
        resolve_count_documents,
        options=[
            Option('search', String, default=None),
            Option('folder_id', Integer, default=None),
            Option('status_id', Integer, default=None),
            Option('signatures_to_finish', Integer, default=None),
            Option('gte', String, default=None),
            Option('lte', String, default=None),
        ]),
```

Works as expected for `{ count_documents }` query without `QueryValidator`, but returns,

```
{
    'data': None,
    'errors': [
        {'message': 'Required option "root.count_documents:search" is not specified'},
        {'message': 'Required option "root.count_documents:folder_id" is not specified'},
        {'message': 'Required option "root.count_documents:status_id" is not specified'},
        {'message': 'Required option "root.count_documents:signatures_to_finish" is not specified'},
        {'message': 'Required option "root.count_documents:gte" is not specified'},
        {'message': 'Required option "root.count_documents:lte" is not specified'}
    ]
}
```

when Hiku handler contains,

```py
        validator = QueryValidator(graph)
        validator.visit(query)
        if validator.errors.list:
            return web.json_response({'data': None, 'errors': ...})
```

----

Right now it is possible to handle this case by providing non-null, but some empty values for arguments of `count_documents` field (like `''`) or by omitting `QueryValidator` at all, but I'd prefer if Hiku works with GraphQL arguments due to specification and does not enforce all arguments to be required.

issue_title: set_bedtools_path() not effective with  pybedtools.BedTool()
issue_body:
Related to #218 , I'm using the master branch with the fix but  I'm still getting the same issue of a `NotImplementedError` (`importlib` fix didn't work for me). 

It's come up for a sorting use case, so the example below uses that, but the reload that should now happen from `helpers.set_bedtools_path()` only seems to work when invoking  `pybedtools.bedtool.BedTool()` rather than  `pybedtools.BedTool()`. I'm not clear on the difference between the two however!  Using `example_filename()` doesn't cause this issue which is why I think it gets through the test cases.
When bedtools is *not* on the path, the first try case will fail:

```python
import pybedtools

a_bed_copy_location = "a_unsorted.bed"
bedtools_location = '/path/to/bedtools/bin/'

# show bedtools path
print("Before set_bedtools_path",pybedtools.helpers.get_bedtools_path())
pybedtools.helpers.set_bedtools_path(bedtools_location)
print("After set_bedtools_path",pybedtools.helpers.get_bedtools_path())

try:
    print("# pybedtools.BedTool")
    bed_file = pybedtools.BedTool(a_bed_copy_location)
    print(bed_file)
    sorted_bed_file = bed_file.sort()
    print("Sorted using pybedtools.BedTool")
    print(sorted_bed_file)
except NotImplementedError:
    print("Cannot sort using pybedtools.BedTool")

print("#####################")


try:
    print("# pybedtools.bedtool.BedTool")
    bed_file = pybedtools.bedtool.BedTool(a_bed_copy_location)
    print(bed_file)
    sorted_bed_file = bed_file.sort()
    print("Sorted using pybedtools.bedtool.BedTool")
    print(sorted_bed_file)

except NotImplementedError:
    print("Cannot sort using pybedtools.bedtool.BedTool")
```

issue_title: cursor._session is None when iterating over cursor directly
issue_body:
When running something like:

``` python
with pytds.connect(as_dict=True, **settings.mssql) as conn:
    with conn.cursor() as cursor:
        cursor.callproc(self.SP_NAME)
        for row in cursor:
            print(row)
```

I'm getting a `'NoneType' object has no attribute 'fetchone'` from the `_session` being None

``` python
> /usr/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pytds/__init__.py(744)fetchone()
-> row = self._session.fetchone()
(Pdb) self._session is None
True
```

For now, the workaround is to iterate over `cursor.fetchall()`, but I think iterating over the cursor directly should not raise that exception

issue_title: DB-API 2.0 places restrictions on types in cursor.description

I learned while comparing the results of a query with the results from `pymssql` that the `cursor.description` data structure is required to have objects in the second (`type_code`) position in each column tuple that compare equal to certain type objects: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#type-objects-and-constructors.

issue_title: EventualResult.wait() can add up to 50ms of latency in Python <3.2
issue_body:
Condition variables in versions of Python earlier than 3.2 use `sleep()` with an exponential backoff for the `wait()` method.  The maximum backoff value is 50ms, meaning that there can be up to 50ms of latency between `notify()`ing a condition variable and a `wait()` returning:

https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/2.6/Lib/threading.py#l243

This is problematic because `EventualResult` uses a condition variable to signal completion (via `threading.Event`), leading to up to 50ms of additional latency.  We've observed this performance issue in production.

issue_title: Computing angle between two rotations
issue_body:
I have two rotation matrices and I want to calculate the angle required to rotate one matrix into the other. To do so, I convert the two rotation matrices into quaternions `q1` and `q2`, compute `q = inv(q1) * q2` and then compute the norm of the vector that results when converting `q` into a rotation vector. With this implementation - while it technically returns a correct result - I noticed that the computed angle does not correspond to the shortest possible rotation. To check this, I also compute the angle of rotation with OpenCV's `Rodrigues` formula (see following code snippet which is a bit lengthy but self-contained).

```
import numpy as np
import quaternion
import cv2


def rad2deg(v):
    """
    Convert from radians to degrees.
    """
    return v * 180.0 / np.pi


def conj(qs):
    """Computes the conjugate of the quaternions, i.e. negates the imaginary parts."""
    return np.conjugate(qs)


def norm(qs):
    """Computes the length of the quaternions, i.e. the sum of the squares of the real and imaginary parts."""
    qs_r = np.reshape(qs, [-1])
    qs_arr = quaternion.as_float_array(qs_r)
    norms = np.sum(qs_arr * qs_arr, axis=-1)
    return np.reshape(norms, qs.shape)


def invert(qs):
    """Inverts the quaternions, i.e. returns the normalized conjugates."""
    conj_ = quaternion.as_float_array(conj(qs))
    normalized = conj_ / norm(qs)[..., np.newaxis]
    return quaternion.as_quat_array(normalized)


def Rx(angle):
    return np.array([[1.0, 0.0, 0.0],
                     [0.0, np.cos(angle), -np.sin(angle)],
                     [0.0, np.sin(angle), np.cos(angle)]])

def Ry(angle):
    return np.array([[np.cos(angle), 0.0, np.sin(angle)],
                     [0.0, 1.0, 0.0],
                     [-np.sin(angle), 0.0, np.cos(angle)]])

def Rz(angle):
    return np.array([[np.cos(angle), -np.sin(angle), 0.0],
                     [np.sin(angle), np.cos(angle), 0.0 ],
                     [0.0, 0.0, 1.0]])

def angle_error_q(rots1, rots2):
    q1 = quaternion.from_rotation_matrix(rots1)
    q2 = quaternion.from_rotation_matrix(rots2)

    q = invert(q1) * q2
    angles = np.linalg.norm(quaternion.as_rotation_vector(q), axis=-1)

    return rad2deg(angles)


def angle_error_rodrigues(rots1, rots2):
    rots2t = np.transpose(rots2, [0, 2, 1])
    E = np.matmul(rots1, rots2t)

    angles = []
    for i in range(E.shape[0]):
        r, _ = cv2.Rodrigues(E[i])
        angles.append(np.linalg.norm(r))
    return rad2deg(np.array(angles))


rots1 = np.array([Rx(np.pi),
                  np.matmul(Ry(np.pi), Rx(np.pi)),
                  np.matmul(Rz(np.pi), Ry(np.pi)),
                  Rx(np.pi/2),
                  Ry(np.pi/4),
                  Rz(np.pi/8)])

rots2 = np.array([Rx(np.pi/2),
                  Ry(np.pi/2),
                  np.matmul(Rz(np.pi), Ry(np.pi)),
                  Rx(-np.pi),
                  Ry(-np.pi),
                  Rz(-np.pi)])

print('with quaternions: ', angle_error_q(rots1, rots2))
print('with Rodrigues:   ', angle_error_rodrigues(rots1, rots2))
```

This prints
```
with quaternions:  [ 270.   180.     0.   270.   225.   202.5]
with Rodrigues:    [  90.   180.     0.    90.   135.   157.5]
```

Is this expected behaviour? Or is may be my implementation of the inverse false? As I said, the output is technically correct, however for my purposes I need the shortest possible angle of rotation as I need the value for an error metric.

It might also have something to do with the following observation. To find out more about what was going on, I installed [this other quaternion library](http://kieranwynn.github.io/pyquaternion/). I then construct a quaternion from the same rotation matrix with both your and this other package and print the result:

```
import numpy as np
import quaternion
from pyquaternion import Quaternion

Q1 = Quaternion(matrix=Rx(np.pi))
q1 = quaternion.from_rotation_matrix(Rx(np.pi))

print(Q1)
print(quaternion.as_float_array(q1)[0])
```

This prints
```
0.000 +1.000i +0.000j +0.000k
[ 0. -1. -0. -0.]
```

So, may be you are assuming a different convention in your implementation? If so, what do I have to do to retrieve the shortest angle of rotation (i.e. essentially get the same result as with the Rodrigues formula)?

issue_title: Make tag first argument to deploy and update commands
Certain routing entries not removed when IP address is deleted from an interface
issue_body:
Run the code to re-produce the error:
```
from pyroute2 import IPDB
import os

ip = IPDB()

interface = ip.create(ifname="test0", kind="dummy")
try:
    interface.add_ip("192.168.71.1/24")
    interface.up()
    interface.commit()
    ip.routes.add(dst="192.168.72.0/24", gateway="192.168.71.254").commit()

    interface.del_ip("192.168.71.1/24").commit()
    interface.down()
    interface.commit()

    for route in ip.routes:
        if route.oif == interface.index:
            print(route["gateway"], route["dst"])
    print("---")
    os.system("ip route show dev test0")
finally:
    interface.remove().commit()
```
What it does it the following:
1. Configures a dummy interface with one IP address
1. Sets a routing with this interface as output
1. Removes the IP addresses from the interface and pulls it down.

The above are working fine, but when a routing table is queried, pyroute2 reports the routing entry created in step 2, but in fact it does not exists due to step 3. As a proof, the script gets the routing table by the ip command where it is confirmed that there's no routing for the dummy interface at all.

What I would expect is that the routing entries in pyroute2 are up-to-date.

issue_title: Spooler fails if message is too long
issue_body:
In SpoolerTask, when message is too long, args and kwargs parameters are passed in body parameter, which is ok. 
```python
if len(repr(message_dict)) >= UWSGI_MAXIMUM_MESSAGE_SIZE:
   # message too long for spooler - we have to use `body` parameter
   message_dict = base_message_dict
   message_dict['body'] = pickle.dumps({
      'args': self.args,
      'kwargs': self.kwargs,
})
```
The problem is that the message is not cleaned from original args/kwargs params (`message_dict['args']` and `message_dict['kwargs']`), and in the result these are not encoded and passed to `uwsgi.spool` function.
In the end `uwsgi.spool` fails with 
> ValueError: spooler callable dictionary must contains only bytes.

issue_title: Support for postgres ArrayField
issue_body:
Django >= 1.8 supports the _ArrayField_ of django.contrib.postgres.fields. I'm using it as an array of strings that's serliazed with drf as _MultipleChoiceField_.

#models.py
`array_field = ArrayField(models.CharField(choices=TYPES, max_length=3))`

#serlializers.py
`array_field = serializers.MultipleChoiceField(choices=TYPES)`

When generating the swagger doc, I hit the following error:
    Exception Type: AttributeError at /swagger/
    **Exception Value: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'get'**

The issue is that _get_basic_type_info_ returns None [here](https://github.com/axnsan12/drf-yasg/blob/fb240f6a5b304fe14a059c9c7afb70124eb85319/src/drf_yasg/inspectors/field.py#L413).

Adding _ArrayField_ to _model_field_to_basic_type_ works for me but I haven't thoroughly tested it. 
```
from django.contrib.postgres.fields import ArrayField
...
model_field_to_basic_type = [
    (models.AutoField, (openapi.TYPE_INTEGER, None)),
    (models.BinaryField, (openapi.TYPE_STRING, openapi.FORMAT_BINARY)),
    (models.BooleanField, (openapi.TYPE_BOOLEAN, None)),
    (models.NullBooleanField, (openapi.TYPE_BOOLEAN, None)),

    (models.IntegerField, (openapi.TYPE_INTEGER, None)),
    (models.IPAddressField, (openapi.TYPE_STRING, openapi.FORMAT_IPV4)),
    (models.GenericIPAddressField, (openapi.TYPE_STRING, openapi.FORMAT_IPV6)),
    (models.SlugField, (openapi.TYPE_STRING, openapi.FORMAT_SLUG)),
    (models.TextField, (openapi.TYPE_STRING, None)),
    (models.TimeField, (openapi.TYPE_STRING, None)),
    (models.UUIDField, (openapi.TYPE_STRING, openapi.FORMAT_UUID)),
    (models.CharField, (openapi.TYPE_STRING, None)),
    (ArrayField, (openapi.TYPE_STRING, openapi.TYPE_ARRAY)),
]
```

The generated ymal looks like this:
```
      array_field:

        items:

```

Do you see any issue adding this support for Django >= 1.8? 

Thanks for the project, it has been really useful.

issue_title: Handling exit code 81
issue_body:
If user installation files do not already exist, the first time LibreOffice starts it will create the files and then immediately quit with exit code 81 (I'm not sure about OpenOffice, but I assume it works similarly). In this case, unoconv will fail, because the listener will not remain open long enough to be used. This behaviour will happen repeatedly if the `env:UserInstallation` variable is set when launching soffice.

I have tested out this scenario in two environments (Mac OSX 10.9 and Ubuntu 12.04). On the mac, unoconv will launch the executable `soffice`, whereas on Ubuntu, it launches `soffice.bin` (this file is not present on MacOSX, at least not with LO 4.2). As far as I can tell, the difference between `soffice` and `soffice.bin` is that `soffice` is a wrapper around `soffice.bin` (where available), and includes logic to automatically restart the executable when an exit code 81 is encountered. Therefore, on MacOSX, I do not run into the problem of unoconv failing because of exit code 81, but on ubuntu I do.

As mentioned, `soffice` (no `.bin`) is present on Ubuntu as well (again, at least for LO 4.2), and using that executable instead of `soffice.bin` seems to have no adverse effects. Does anyone know why the choice was made to use `soffice.bin` instead?

issue_title: Improve Unicode name matching regex
issue_body:
Great that you're already matching Unicode names! However, looks like you're currently simply accepting one-or-more characters of class `w`:
```py3
Name = r'\w+'
```
AFAICT, a better regex for this would be:
```py3
Name = r'[^\d\W]\w*'
```

issue_title: kafka-python 1.3.2 KafkaConsumer not working with gevent
issue_body:
Running gevent 1.1.1 on python 3.5.0 with kafka-python 1.3.2... Unable to connect a KafkaConsumer to my brokers. Here is a sample script:

```
from gevent import monkey
monkey.patch_all()

from kafka import KafkaConsumer
k = KafkaConsumer(bootstrap_servers=["remotehost1:9092", "remotehost2:9092"], group_id=None)
```

 and I receive the following error:

`ERROR:kafka.conn:Connect attempt to <BrokerConnection host=xxxxxxx port=9092> returned error [Errno 115] Operation now in progress. Disconnecting.`

Digging deeper it seems like it stems from the fact that the library manually controls the blocking state of the socket, which makes it incompatible with gevent (see here: https://github.com/gevent/gevent/issues/670). Stack trace from the socket error below:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 20, in <module>
    group_id=None)
  File "/home/asgoel/Documents//venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/kafka/consumer/group.py", line 284, in __init__
    self._client = KafkaClient(metrics=self._metrics, **self.config)
  File "/home/asgoel/Documents//venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/kafka/client_async.py", line 197, in __init__
    self._bootstrap(collect_hosts(self.config['bootstrap_servers']))
  File "/home/asgoel/Documents//venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/kafka/client_async.py", line 227, in _bootstrap
    bootstrap.connect()
  File "/home/asgoel/Documents//venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/kafka/conn.py", line 266, in connect
    ret = self._sock.connect_ex((self.host, self.port))
  File "/home/asgoel/Documents//venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/gevent/_socket3.py", line 291, in connect_ex
    return self.connect(address) or 0
  File "/home/asgoel/Documents//venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/gevent/_socket3.py", line 265, in connect
    return _socket.socket.connect(self._sock, address)
BlockingIOError: [Errno 115] Operation now in progress
```

Are there any plans to support gevent in the near future? Or any ways you can think of to get around this? a lot of our existing code depends on gevent so would love to be able to integrate smoothly.

issue_body:
In Ansible 2.x the Python API has changed, the relevant docs are [here](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/d9d4ad828a7d3f9f1440e4bec7150756165b754b/docsite/rst/developing_api.rst). 

A check is made in [L21-27 of ansible.py](https://github.com/philpep/testinfra/blob/master/testinfra/backend/ansible.py#L21-L27) to try and import `ansible.runner` and an variable `HAS_ANSIBLE` is set to True or False as a result.

```
try:
    import ansible.inventory
    import ansible.runner
except ImportError:
    HAS_ANSIBLE = False
else:
    HAS_ANSIBLE = True
```
The module `ansible.runner` has been removed from Ansible 2.x (a PITA I know).

[Later](https://github.com/philpep/testinfra/blob/master/testinfra/backend/ansible.py#L42-L46) a check is made on this variable, if it is set to False the program bails out with an error 'You must install ansible package to use the ansible backend'.

issue_title: Disable fuzzy strings
issue_body:
Having two different kinds of translations (fuzzy, and untranslated) confuses new translators in our code-base. So we would like to disable the fuzziness matching, and show all strings as untranslated instead. So this is a feature request for a setting that does this.

Ideally, this should be done in Django, but they have firmly stated that feature as wontfix: https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/10852 - so I'm hoping you will take up this usability issue instead :)

issue_title: Improve Excel data fields
issue_body:
Currently Excel date fields only support the format YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss and use some heuristics to decide if it is a date or time only fields.

Actually Excel supports custom formats, as described in for example https://www.ablebits.com/office-addins-blog/2015/03/11/change-date-format-excel/#custom-date-format. Most of them can be mapped to to a cutplace DateTime format.

issue_title: Include sys.version in --version output
issue_body: To assist with debugging and confirm the expected Python interpreter version is being used, it would be helpful to include `sys.version` in the output of `--version`.

issue_title: Shutdown sharded nodes in reverse order (compared to startup)
issue_body:
Currently nodes are shutdown in (potentially) random order:

https://github.com/rueckstiess/mtools/blob/1.1.8/mtools/mlaunch/mlaunch.py#L422-L433

This makes it possible for the config server to be stopped before the mongos.  If auth is on, this means that the mongos shutdown cannot auth before issuing the `shutdown` command, causing an error such as:

```
Error: cannot authenticate to shut down localhost:11111.
```

Even though there is apparently nothing wrong with auth or the credentials.

**Sharded nodes should be shutdown in reverse order to how they were started up, i.e. mongoses, shard mongods, config mongods.**

issue_title: Disk read/write stats in diskdata isn't computed correctly when a disk is specified
issue_body:
When a disk is specified, a logic error in _get_io_stats() results in statistics for all drives being summed, rather than just the selected drive.

Additionally, the filtering is a simple string match, so specifying "sda" or "/dev/sda" will sum stats for "sda", "sda1", "sda2", etc resulting in incorrect values.

Pull request is incoming momentarily to fix both of these issues.

issue_title: Drop Python 3.3 support
Segfault in authGSSClientWrap caused by freeing a stack allocated buffer (pykerberos >= 1.1.6)
issue_body:
The regression occurred in version 1.1.6 with the following commit:  https://github.com/02strich/pykerberos/commit/1de650c804b015f880310d988da2fb6caa2e362f.

The author of that patch added a call to gss_release_buffer on input_token.value at the end of the function:

https://github.com/02strich/pykerberos/blob/v1.1.10/src/kerberosgss.c#L606-L607

The problem is that input_token.value is a stack allocated buffer:

https://github.com/02strich/pykerberos/blob/v1.1.10/src/kerberosgss.c#L560-L576

If authGSSClientWrap is called with the "user" option the interpreter segfaults. Here is the backtrace from gdb (with sensitive data redacted):

```
(gdb) bt
#0  0x00007f93bb0e7297 in raise () from /lib64/libc.so.6
#1  0x00007f93bb0e862a in abort () from /lib64/libc.so.6
#2  0x00007f93bb125ce0 in __libc_message () from /lib64/libc.so.6
#3  0x00007f93bb12b19e in malloc_printerr () from /lib64/libc.so.6
#4  0x00007f93bb12b98b in _int_free () from /lib64/libc.so.6
#5  0x00007f93b9a9abf8 in gss_release_buffer () from /usr/lib64/libgssapi_krb5.so.2
#6  0x00007f93b9cd0cbc in authenticate_gss_client_wrap (state=0xdf1560, challenge=0x7f93affc80e4 "redacted", 
    user=0x7f93b506cbd4 "redacted", protect=0) at src/kerberosgss.c:607
#7  0x00007f93b9cce8c3 in authGSSClientWrap (self=0x0, 
    args=(<PyCObject at remote 0x7f93b5067328>, 'redacted', u'redacted')) at src/kerberos.c:318
```

Remove the gss_release_buffer call. It would also be useful to add a test for authGSSClientUnwrap and authGSSClientWrap to avoid future regressions.

issue_title: CLI Error using input TXT
issue_body:
Ok I have 2 bug's to mention

This is the first one:

When downloading stories using a txt, depending on the last character of the input, the CLI might not download the correct story.
Specificaly if the last line of the input txt has the url:
> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515
the cli will instead try to download 
> http://archiveofourown.org/works/483351

Similarly if the last line is the url:
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678
it will try to download
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/1151567
and fail since that story does not exist.

On the other hand urls like 
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/1/This-Bites
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/1/
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/
and
> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515/chapters/11070479
> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515/
are fine because in those cases the url to be downloaded basically becomes
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/1/This-Bite
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678/1
> https://www.fanfiction.net/s/11515678
and
> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515/chapters/1107047
> http://archiveofourown.org/works/4833515
and those redirect to the correct stories.

**Note:** The above obviously applies if the file does _not_ end in a newline. If there _is_ a newline then obviously that newline is the last character in the txt and it is that newline that is deleted

issue_title: Don't collapse instruments by program number
issue_body:
When loading in a MIDI file, a single `Instrument` instance is created for all events on all channels and all tracks which have the same program number.  This is problematic primarily because pitch bend and other control events will get collapsed onto a single instrument, when they shouldn't be.  For example:

```Python
import pretty_midi
pm = pretty_midi.PrettyMIDI()
i = pretty_midi.Instrument(0)
i.notes.append(pretty_midi.Note(100, 36, 0.0, 1.0))
i.notes.append(pretty_midi.Note(100, 40, 0.0, 1.0))
i.notes.append(pretty_midi.Note(100, 45, 0.0, 1.0))
pm.instruments.append(i)
i = pretty_midi.Instrument(0)
i.notes.append(pretty_midi.Note(100, 66, 0.0, 1.0))
for n in range(8000):
    i.pitch_bends.append(pretty_midi.PitchBend(n, (n + 10)/8000.))
pm.instruments.append(i)
pm.write('test.mid')
pm2 = pretty_midi.PrettyMIDI('test.mid')
print len(pm2.instruments)
print len(pm2.instruments[0].notes)
print len(pm2.instruments[0].pitch_bends)
```
yields
```
1
4
8000
```
but
```Python
import midi
print len(midi.read_midifile('test.mid'))
```
yields 3. I.e., the net result is that when loading in this MIDI file which has 3 tracks (one timing track, one "chord" track with no pitch bends, and one "single note" track with a single note with many pitch bends) is that the two instrument tracks get merged into one `Instrument` which has all four notes and all pitch bends - meaning that the "chord" gets pitch bent, when it shouldn't.  So, we should create separate `Instrument` instances for all channels and tracks.

issue_title: wxPython's GetHandle() returns 0 with wxPython 3.0/4.0 on Linux
issue_body:
On Linux with wxPython 3.x / 4.x window handle is not yet available even though Show() was called and it causes in the wxpython.py example to open two windows, one blank window and another window with CEF browser. This effect is because window handle passed to CEF is 0, thus CEF creates a top-level window on its own.

The solution is to delay embedding browser by a bit and window handle becomes available then. Time of 1ms is not enough, 10ms seems fine, but to be sure I've used 20ms. Example works fine with such code:

```
        # On Linux must show before embedding browser, so that handle
        # is available (Issue #347).
        if LINUX:
            self.Show()
            # In wxPython 3.0 and wxPython 4.0 handle is still
            # not yet available, must delay embedding browser
            # (Issue #349).
            if wx.version().startswith("3.") or wx.version().startswith("4."):
                wx.CallLater(20, self.embed_browser)
            else:
                # This works fine in wxPython 2.8
                self.embed_browser()
        else:
            self.embed_browser()
            self.Show()
```

Issue originally reported on the Forum:
https://groups.google.com/d/topic/cefpython/ZBXEdOR3svw/discussion

issue_title: Simplify model state representation
issue_body:
Right now, there's three different types a model state can be: `NoneType`, an array, or a tuple of arrays. In the future, it would be nice to unify this.

I propose that states will _always_ be tuples. An empty tuple corresponds to a stateless model; otherwise, the tuple may contain one element (the standard state case), or more than one element. Also, instead of a `stateful` property, there could just be a `num_states` property that indicates how many tuple elements are in the state.

issue_title: Add a description of what is/not working
issue_body:
Some uses of uinput are not currently supported (for example, rumblers: #23, [ff branch](/gvalkov/python-evdev/tree/ff)).  Which, doesn't seem to be documented.

Adding to the documentation, a brief description of what is and isn't working, might help potential users anticipate whether python-evdev is appropriate for their intended use.

Here is a strawman draft:
## Scope and status

All keyboard and mice events should work.  Rumblers #23 currently do not work.  Joysticks, touchpads, tablets, cameras, force feedback, and less common devices *probably work / probably don't work / sometimes work / partially work / haven't been tested / are not supported*.

Some characters, such as `:` (colon), cannot easily be generated from scratch #7.  Translating them into UInput events would require knowing the kernel keyboard translation table, and there is no python equivalent of `dumpkeys`.  Consider using [PyUserInput](https://github.com/PyUserInput/PyUserInput) instead.

Related projects include: [python-uinput](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/python-uinput/); [uinput-mapper](https://github.com/MerlijnWajer/uinput-mapper); [PyUserInput](https://github.com/PyUserInput/PyUserInput); [pygame](http://www.pygame.org/).

issue_title: published_parsed is wrong sometimes
issue_body:
```python
In [27]: f['entries'][0]["published"]
Out[27]: u'2016/6/29 15:07:41'

In [28]: f['entries'][0]["published_parsed"]
Out[28]: time.struct_time(tm_year=2016, tm_mon=6, tm_mday=1, tm_hour=0, tm_min=0, tm_sec=0, tm_wday=2, tm_yday=153, tm_isdst=0)

In [29]: datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(time.mktime(f['entries'][0]["published_parsed"]))
Out[29]: datetime.datetime(2016, 6, 1, 0, 0)
```
as we can see, feedparser parsed `"2016/6/29 15:07:41"` into `datetime.datetime(2016, 6, 1, 0, 0)`.    
After reading the related code roughly,  i found `_parse_date_iso8601` was used to parse the date.  The problem is that `2016/6/29 15:07:41` is not in iso8601 format.
The re pattern used in `_parse_date_iso8601` and the result returned:
```python
In [20]: m = re.match("(?P<year>\d{4})(T?(?P<hour>\d{2}):(?P<minute>\d{2})(:(?P<second>\d{2}))?(\.(?P<fracsecond>\d+))?(?P<tz>[+-](?P<tzhour>\d{2})(:(?P<tzmin>\d{2}))?|Z)?)?", '2016/6/29 13:15:50')
In [22]: params = m.groupdict()
In [23]: params
Out[23]:
{'fracsecond': None,
 'hour': None,
 'minute': None,
 'second': None,
 'tz': None,
 'tzhour': None,
 'tzmin': None,
 'year': '2016'}
```
The re pattern used here can only get the year out and the code try to make assumption to the month and day.  Why trying to make assumption? So the `published_parsed` can not be trusted?

issue_title: feed entry published date should have better defaults
issue_body:
There are two possible fallbacks for the feed date that are missing:

* the entry's `created` date field
* the *feed* itself's `updated` date field

in my code, I worked around this by doing this in a loop when processing entries:

```python
        # add more defaults to entry dates:
        # 1. created_parsed of the item
        # 2. updated_parsed of the feed
        entry['updated_parsed'] = entry.get('updated_parsed', entry.get('created_parsed', data['feed'].get('updated_parsed', False)))  # noqa
        assert entry.get('updated_parsed') is not None
```

quite a mouthful... it seems to me the entry getter should at least add `created` as a fallback, but also use the feed's updated date as a fallback as well... 

is there a better way to do this? or to monkeypatch around that issue in a cleaner way?

thanks!

issue_title: get_jobs() doc and warning have conflicts
issue_body:
The docstring reads:

> Returns a list of pending jobs (if the scheduler hasn't been started yet) and scheduled jobs, either from a specific job store or from all of them.
> If the scheduler has not been started yet, only pending jobs can be returned because the
job stores haven't been started yet either.

And the warning in this method says:
```
warnings.warn('The "pending" option is deprecated -- get_jobs() always returns '
                          'pending jobs if the scheduler has been started and scheduled jobs '
                          'otherwise', DeprecationWarning)
```

As I read it, I think `pending jobs` means those scheduled but not run even once, and `scheduled jobs` means those running ones, right? So the docstring makes sense but the warning means the opposite, thus the confusion. You may miss a `not` in warning? I don't PR because I want to confirm that point.

issue_title: WeekdayPositionExpression: unexpected keyword argument 'option' and 'weekday'
issue_body:
The Python (2.5) report following error:
  File "C:\programy.win\Python25\lib\site-packages\apscheduler\triggers.py", lin
e 35, in compile_single
    
    #!python
        return compiler(**match.groupdict())
    TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'option'
    

Solution:
Original code with error:

    
    #!python
    
    class WeekdayPositionExpression(object):
        options = ['1st', '2nd', '3rd', '4th', '5th', 'last']
        value_re = re.compile(r'(?P<option>%s) +(?P<weekday>(?:\d+|\w+))'
                              % '|'.join(options), re.IGNORECASE)
    

Fixed line:

    
    #!python
        value_re = re.compile(r'(?P<**option_name**>%s) +(?P<weekday_name>(?:\d+|\w+))'
                              % '|'.join(options), re.IGNORECASE)

issue_title: Mingus 0.6: classes and mixins
issue_body:
I've been working on a OO redesign of mingus which is slowly progressing into something usable (see the files in the `mingus/` root on the [mingus-oo branch](https://github.com/bspaans/python-mingus/tree/mingus-oo/mingus)). The new code is hopefully more user-friendly and easier to program, and gets rid off the disconnect between the procedural style of `mingus.core` and the clunky OO style of `mingus.containers`. 

I started mingus as a side project whilst in uni to explore the algorithms behind music theory, which basically grew into `mingus.core`. A bit later on I wanted to hook the notes generated by mingus up into a midi synthesizer for which I ended up needing a bunch of container classes. The design that emerged has been a bit of a pain to maintain and work with ever since.

As an example: in `core` we can use a note to build intervals, chords and scales, but in `containers` we don't have that ability and we need to get the note name out first and then convert the result back into a relevant container. This could be remedied in the current code base, but the fact remains that there are two different representations for notes, and this is excluding the conversion to MIDI and LilyPond, etc. which complicate matters even further. So in short: there is a usability problem; we have duplication, complicated conversions, etc.

The new design sort of merges core and containers together and uses mixins to provide most of the behaviour. It also prefers immutable operations over mutable ones, which makes it easier to do procedural generation. The new design is not only cleaner but also makes it easy to implement some  new features like the ability to build chords and progressions on top of arbitrary scales.

Unfortunately there's quite a bit to do before this can be released as a replacement for mingus 0.5: 

* finish porting, refactoring and testing
* rewrite the documentation 
* describe the upgrade process/provide backwards compatibility

I don't have a lot of time to spend on mingus these days, but this is the direction I want to take the project in. Feedback welcome.

issue_title: Allow modification of parsing grammar at runtime, without reloading the entire grammar
issue_body:
Imagine if you would, a whole sea of users naively trying to improve their forks of a base grammar all on the same server where each grammar runs in a mostly idle state on occasional sporadic user-generated input. The more users that have to reload the entire grammar they've been working on, the more the server might bog down. It would be considerably better if only the changes to the grammars needed to be loaded while the grammars worked. Is this at all doable?

Also, on the general subject of extensibility, rules are going to have to be added to the head of the grammar, the tail of the grammar, but also inserted into precise places in the middle of the grammar, because PEG requires prioritized order like that; probably by referencing a rule above or below which to insert a new rule.

Might I suggest something like:
grammar=Grammar ("""
    a="A"
    d="D"
    """)
grammar.insert ("""b="B"""" below "a")
grammar.insert ("""c="C"""" above "d")

Also, to delete a rule:
grammar.delete ("d")

And, so grammar editors can know what the currently running grammar looks like:
grammar.print ("optional_rule")

issue_title: -i option requires a file. directories not supported
issue_body:
First,    thanks for the awesome tool.  I've been looking for a way to visualize my infrastructure for a while now.  I love this idea.

On to the issue:
ansible allows "inventory" to point at a directory, it then parses all files in that directory to create the inventory list. As such, I've broken my hosts into a directory like so:

hosts/
hosts/development
hosts/test
hosts/staging
hosts/production

and in ansible.cfg, I have:
inventory = ./hosts

The problem is,  ansible-cmdb doesn't allow me to specify -i as a directory,   or multiple -i statements (one per file),  thus the resulting html output is partial and incomplete.

issue_title: Functions for converting other SVG primitives to paths not importable
issue_body:
We need the functionality used in the primitive conversion functions: `ellipse2pathd`, `polyline2pathd`, `polygon2pathd` and `rect2pathd`

Unfortunately, as `svgpathtools.__init__` exposes a function with the same name as the module `svg2paths`, importing these externally is not possible.

Would it be possible to allow these to be importable? I am happy to do any refactoring required if this is a change you would accept. At the moment we have the code copy / pasted in our repo, and would be nicer to avoid this duplication.

issue_title: Possibility to pass an explicit list of bins to `df.iplot(kind='hist', ...)`
issue_body:
See [this question on SE](https://stackoverflow.com/q/45784591/4063051) for major details.

Basically, it seems that the `bins` parameter only accepts an integer, to be interpreted as the total number of bins in the plot. This is a problem when plotting multiple histograms with different data ranges because they get binned indipendently and thus often inconsistently (see above post).

This seems to be confirmed in (what seems to me to be) the [relevant section of the code](https://github.com/santosjorge/cufflinks/blob/ed3e666a017c869ef1c85a05dcdae5e222509b5e/cufflinks/plotlytools.py#L829-L833).

Is there another intended way to explicitly set the bins, like can be done in `matplotlib`/`pandas` directly with the `bins` parameter, or in `plotly` with `xbins`?

issue_title: Memory usage is wrong (usage vs cache)?
issue_body:
Hi,

It seems that the memory usage reported by the API `memory_stats/usage` is not correct if the container is doing a lot of IO and thus has lots of disk cache.

Example of a mysql database:
```
# docker stats
MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %
329.6MiB / 2.868GiB   11.22%
```

The API shows:
```
check_docker.sh --containers prd-piwik-mysql --memory 500:1000:m
WARNING: prd-piwik-mysql memory is 784.765625m|prd-piwik-mysql_mem=784.765625m;500;1000;0;2936.6015625
```

This seems related to the issue https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/10824

issue_title: Type of BSD license, explicit license file
issue_body:
Hey-lo,

I'm building a version of `sphinx-prompt` using [`conda`](http://conda.pydata.org/) for [conda-forge](http://conda-forge.github.io/). When possible, we try to include a link to the license file in the `meta.yaml` specification for the build; doing so requires the license be indexed in an explicit [`MANIFEST.in`](https://docs.python.org/2/distutils/sourcedist.html#manifest-related-options) file so that it gets included in the source distribution. Also, in the case of BSD licenses, it means that we need to know whether the package is licensed under the [BSD 2-Clause](https://opensource.org/licenses/BSD-2-Clause) or [BSD 3-Clause](https://opensource.org/licenses/BSD-3-Clause) variant.

Would you consider specifying which kind of BSD license you're using, adding a license file to the repo, and including it in the build via `MANIFEST.in`? Thanks very much!

issue_title: No compatible with `__slots__`
issue_body:
Hi. Very interresting project.
I was about to develop the same thing, but made a research first and found this

The problem is that it doesn't support the use of `__slots__`

Demonstration:
```python
>>> from cached_property import cached_property
>>> class C:
...     __slots__ = 'x',
...     @cached_property
...     def y(self):
...         return 'y'
... 
>>> c = C()
>>> c.y
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/cached_property.py", line 26, in __get__
    value = obj.__dict__[self.func.__name__] = self.func(obj)
AttributeError: 'C' object has no attribute '__dict__'
```

I have a little wrapper for this named `slot_wrapper` [here:](https://github.com/yahya-abou-imran/metannotations/blob/9b6d9406817d64c36ff8e9911429c1dfc2f409cb/metannotations.py#L47)
```
def slot_wrapper(attrname, slot, Descr, cls):

    class InnerDescr(Descr):

        def __get__(self, obj, cls):
            return slot.__get__(obj, cls)

        def __set__(self, obj, val):
            slot.__set__(obj, val)

return InnerDescr(attrname, cls)
```
It takes the descriptor under the slot and replace it (__slots__ are special descriptors, but they remains descriptors).
It needs adjustments with your code in order to work... I use it with metclass so it got instaciate after the creation of the class.

My demand is between a bug report, a feature request and an enhacement proposal!

issue_title: Misleading datetime in revert message. Easy fix.
issue_body:
Reversion is a wonderful project, nevertheless I found a subtle bug.

The revert action saves the admin log message "Reverted to previous version, saved on <datetime>". When the `settings.USE_TZ = True`, then the code `localize(version.revision.date_created)` makes the timezone-aware datetime object nicely human readable, but cuts off the UTC timezone info.

The fix is how Django renders the datetimes in templates: `localize(timezone.template_localtime(...)`. Thanks.

Consider a high-performance wrapper using the unity build
issue_body:
CRoaring has a unity build:
https://github.com/RoaringBitmap/CRoaring#amalgamationunity-build

This avoids the nagging problem of dealing with a generic static library. Instead, you have a single header file and a single ``.c`` file that can be included directly. 

Using such a unity build, it should be possible to produce a higher-performance wrapper with good inlining. Moreover, the unity build could be included in this repository, making it super easy for people to proceed.

Like the unittest ones, hide the nose ones. They appear when you have top-level functions that are tests, like this:

    FAIL: tests.tests:test_querying_fields
      bbedit +197 /Users/erose/Virtualenvs/elasticutils/lib/python2.6/site-packages/nose/case.py  # runTest
        self.test(*self.arg)
      bbedit +118 tests/tests.py  # test_querying_fields
        eq_(implicit._build_query(), explicit._build_query())
      bbedit +31  /Users/erose/Virtualenvs/elasticutils/lib/python2.6/site-packages/nose/tools.py  # eq_
        assert a == b, msg or "%r != %r" % (a, b)

We're smart enough to find the test frame to highlight it, so let's just skip everything before that and save screen space and reading. We should make the frame guesser more conservative, as right now it defaults to the last frame.

issue_title: is_dirty() return False but object has been modified…
issue_body:
I have two models, `Assembly` and `Location`.  An assembly may contain other assemblies so when a location changes, I want to make sure the location of all the sub-assemblies changes as well. The code to do this is:

```
    def save(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """Additions to the default abstract save method."""
        try:
            import ipdb; ipdb.set_trace()  # Used to get debugging info.
            if self.is_dirty(check_relationship=True):
                dirties = self.get_dirty_fields(check_relationship=True)
                if 'locality' in dirties.keys():
                    self.ripple_downwards('locality', self.locality)
        finally:
            super(Assembly, self).save(*args, **kwargs)
```

This used to work fine with `dirtyfields` version 0.8.2. However, with 1.1 it does not. Here is some debugging information:

```
ipdb> self.pk
1
ipdb> sut = Assembly.objects.get(pk=1)
ipdb> sut.pk == self.pk
True
ipdb> self.locality
<Location: Afghanistan>
ipdb> sut.locality is None
True
ipdb> self.get_dirty_fields(check_relationship=True)
{}
```

Am I doing something wrong?

TypeError: int() argument must be a string or a number, not 'NoneType'
```

It would seem `_check_sane_and_get_gpg_version` is not fully sanitizing the version nor aborting when it cannot. I still don't know the root cause of the GPG's problem (the system was experiencing general problems, though). What _appears_ to be happening is that for whatever reason, GPG had a hiccup where it exited cleanly but didn't return a parsable version string.

Below is a transcript of my testing / debugging by running snippets of `gnupg` code.

```
$ gpg --list-config --with-colons

cfg:pubkey:1;2;3;16;17

cfg:digest:1;2;3;8;9;10;11
cfg:digestname:MD5;SHA1;RIPEMD160;SHA256;SHA384;SHA512;SHA224
cfg:compress:0;1;2;3
```

The nominal case.

``` python
$ python3
Python 3.5.1 (default, Dec 26 2015, 18:11:22)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 7.0.2 (clang-700.1.81)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> s='''\
... cfg:version:1.4.20
... cfg:pubkey:1;2;3;16;17
... cfg:cipher:1;2;3;4;7;8;9;10;11;12;13
... cfg:ciphername:IDEA;3DES;CAST5;BLOWFISH;AES;AES192;AES256;TWOFISH;CAMELLIA128;CAMELLIA192;CAMELLIA256

>>> str(s).partition (':version:')[2].split('\n')[0]

If the subprocess returns with a non-zero return code an exception would have been thrown. In this case, it exited with a non-zero, but the partitioning for the version returned something like--'1', '1.4', or '' (the last being the most likely).

Later when it tries to determine if it is GPGv2 (example assumes `''` was set for the version string):

issue_title: Inject creates multiple objects when a singleton was requested...
issue_body:
Ran into some weirdness in using Inject. Weirdness that I consider to be a bug...

I had declared that I wanted only one copy of a certain kind of object - but it gave me two distinct objects.

It took a while to figure out the symptoms in my code were caused by that, and what the underlying cause was.

One of the consumers of these objects was in a certain module, and the other was in the same file as the object declaration.

Because I was testing this new code, I had some code below the 'if __name__ == "__main__" clause.

That code thought the object class was <__main__.Store>, and the other consumers (and the code that set up the injection mapping) thought it was <store.Store>. Although they're the same class, Python treats them as different classes... Hence, any consumers of the object which used inject.params in __main__, were always going to get a unique object each time it asked for one. This happens because the inject code thinks it's OK to instantiate an object (with no parameters) if there is no declared supplier of the object. In my view, that's an error. If I declare that I need a certain kind of object in inject.params, then IMHO it's an error for me not to supply it in the injection parameters.

It would have cut several hours off the process to realize what was going on sooner - with an error message. Especially if the error message said something like: No constructor for object type blah - currently known constructors are: (list of known object types). That would have made it dead obvious what was wrong _immediately_.

So, I think that should be an error, at least by default (maybe all the time?).

Ideally, it would be nice to figure out how to keep this from happening, then you wouldn't have to make that an error.

Here's a thought about one approach:

Upon checking, no matter where you look, cls.__name__ gives the same answer for both __main__.Store and store.store. So, if you paid attention to the class name instead of the actual class, then I believe that would solve the problem.

It seems really unlikely that someone would have two different classes of the same name, and want to supply two different constructors for them.

issue_title: marshal() should support generators and not lists and tuples only

`marshalling.marshal()` handles lists and tuples by marshalling each element and putting the result into a json list. Ideally this should also work with generators: There is no real reason to create multiple lists for just passing them around.

issue_title: _get_or_create_user: user_data["attributes"] - key error
issue_body:
ldap search can return different kinds of responses: 
 - type `searchResEntry` - it contains the `attributes` key, all good
 - type `searchResRef` - `attributes` key is not present, and then `_get_or_create_user` will raise an error.

It's especially valid for `iter_users` when both types can be present.

Quick workaround for `iter_users`:
``` python
return (
    self._get_or_create_user(entry)
    for entry
    in paged_entries if entry["type"] == "searchResEntry"
)
```

issue_title: Unable to exclude many-to-many relationships
issue_body:
This seems to be a bit different from the other many-to-many issues here: I have a many-to-many relationship on a versioned table that I don't want tracked by continuum.

Here's a paired down version of the models:

```
from sqlalchemy import Table, Column, DateTime, String, Integer, ForeignKey, Text, func
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship, backref

from meta import Base 

channel_tag = Table('service_channel_service_tag', Base.metadata,
    Column('channel_id', Integer, ForeignKey('service_channel.id'), primary_key=True),
    Column('tag_id', Integer, ForeignKey('service_tag.id'))
)

class ServiceChannel(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'service_channel'
    __versioned__ = {
        'exclude': ['tags']
    }

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    title = Column(Text(convert_unicode=True))
    description = Column(Text(convert_unicode=True))

    tags = relationship("ServiceTag",
                        secondary=channel_tag,
                        backref="channels")

    def __repr__(self):
        return "<ServiceChannel(id='%s')>" % (self.id)

class ServiceTag(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'service_tag'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    tag = Column(String(30), unique=True, nullable=False)

    def __repr__(self):
        return "<ServiceTag(id='%s', tag='%s')>" % (self.id, self.tag)
```

In this particular case, having the historical data of tags is unnecessary. Is there some obvious way to have continuum ignore this that I'm missing?

issue_title: Enable fallbacks for all models
issue_body:
Hello! First of all thank you for the great project! It really helps to seamlessly create and use models in different languages. 

Now, the question :) 

There are a lot of TranslationModel-s in my app and I need to enable fallbacks to default languages (taken from settings.LANGUAGES) for all of them.

After rtfm-ing I came to the following, a bit ugly solution:

```
class FallbackTranslationQuerySet(manager.TranslationQueryset):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self.fallbacks()
```
... and then for each model:

```
class YetAnotherModel1 (TranslatableModel):
    objects = manager.TranslationManager(default_class=FallbackTranslationQuerySet)
    ...

class YetAnotherModel2(TranslatableModel):
    objects = manager.TranslationManager(default_class=FallbackTranslationQuerySet)
    ...

...
```

Maybe there is some more clean solution?

Thanks!

        """
        Endpoint for allowing a provider to accept a request.
        ---

        response_serializer: snowremovalservice.serializers.RequestSerializer
        parameters_strategy: replace

        parameters:
            - name: pk
              description: Primary key of the request to be accepted.
              required: true
              type: int

    @detail_route(methods=['post'])
    def accept_from_provider(self, request, pk=None):
        """
        Endpoint for allowing a provider to accept a request.

issue_title: Opcode checking needed
issue_body:
After the [report from duskCoder](https://github.com/Pithikos/python-websocket-server/issues/5) it became apparent that no opcode checking is implemented atm. This can be problematic since the user doesn't get any information on PING/PONG for example.

OPCODE checking needs to be implemented in `message_received()` and an appropriate warning needs to be outputted to the user in case of an unknown OPCODE.

issue_body:

Suppose you have the following models.py and translation.py.
Then when I wont to retrieve only published objects using the custom PublishManager, I get all the objects, not only published==True.

```
## translation.py
from modeltranslation.translator import translator,TranslationOptions
from foo.models import Bar

class BarTranslationOptions(TranslationOptions):
    fields = ('text',)

translator.register(Bar, BarTranslationOptions)

##models.py
from django.db import models

class PublishManager(models.Manager):
    def get_query_set(self):
        return super(PublishManager, self).get_query_set().filter(publish=True)

class Foo(models.Model):
    text = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    publish = models.BooleanField(default=True)
    objects = models.Manager()

class Bar(Foo):

issue_title: defaults for `default` and `missing`
issue_body:
I've been thinking this over for a bit now...  I found that what I at first considered a bug is probably better described as a design decision.  I'm looking for input from people to see what they think...

Basically, the colander schema you use differs if you're generating a form for **adding** a new object then if you were **editing** an object.  For example, if you're adding a new object with an incremental primary key, you probably want to have `missing=colander.drop` so that the backend SQL system fills in the value with the next incremental value.  However, if you are modifying/editing that same object it doesn't make as much sense.  In the editing case a person blanking out that field would mean it would make no change to the field, but what you probably want is `missing=colander.required` as you'd want the validator to trip when the person tried to null a primary key field.

Right now ColanderAlchemy has a focus on the right settings for **adding** only.  I was thinking it might be helpful to add an argument that you pass to specify which defaults to use (whether "add" or "edit"), but that's an enhancement for another time.  For right now, I think it's best to continue the focus on "add only" but make it so the schema has as little dissimilarity with the "edit" schema as possible.  To that end, I'd like to make nullable fields set to `missing=colander.null`.

Here's a test case that currently fails that I'd like to make work:
```python
    def test_null_issues(self):
        """ Make sure nullable values are set properly when null
        
        #42 introduced an issue where values may not have been properly
        set if the value is supposed to be set to null
        """
        Base = declarative_base()

        class Person(Base):
            __tablename__ = 'person'
            id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
            fullname = Column(String, nullable=True)
            age = Column(Integer, nullable=True)

        schema = SQLAlchemySchemaNode(Person)

        person = Person(
            id=7,
            fullname="Joe Smith",
            age=35,
        )

        # dict coming from a form submission
        update_cstruct = {
            'id': '7',
            'fullname': '',
            'age': '',
        }
        update_appstruct = schema.deserialize(update_cstruct)
        schema.objectify(update_appstruct, context=person)

        self.assertEqual(person.id, 7)
        self.assertEqual(person.fullname, None)
        self.assertEqual(person.age, None)
```
