Generated code:
```
    def configure_trigger_edge(self, trigger_source, level, slope, trigger_coupling, holdoff, delay):
        '''configure_trigger_edge

        Configures common properties for analog edge triggering.

        When you initiate an acquisition, the digitizer waits for the start
        trigger, which is configured through the ACQ_ARM_SOURCE
        (Start Trigger Source) attribute. The default is immediate. Upon
        receiving the start trigger the digitizer begins sampling pretrigger
        points. After the digitizer finishes sampling pretrigger points, the
        digitizer waits for a reference (stop) trigger that you specify with a
        function such as this one. Upon receiving the reference trigger the
        digitizer finishes the acquisition after completing posttrigger
        sampling. With each Configure Trigger function, you specify
        configuration parameters such as the trigger source and the amount of
        trigger delay.

        Note:
        Some features are not supported by all digitizers. Refer to `Features
        Supported by
        Device <REPLACE_DRIVER_SPECIFIC_URL_1(features_supported_main)>`__ for
        more information.

        Args:
            trigger_source (string): Specifies the trigger source. Refer to TRIGGER_SOURCE
                for defined values.
            level (float): The voltage threshold for the trigger. Refer to
                TRIGGER_LEVEL for more information.
            slope (enums.TriggerSlope): Specifies whether you want a rising edge or a falling edge to trigger
                the digitizer. Refer to TRIGGER_SLOPE for more
                information.
            trigger_coupling (int): Applies coupling and filtering options to the trigger signal. Refer to
                TRIGGER_COUPLING for more information.
            holdoff (float): The length of time the digitizer waits after detecting a trigger before
                enabling NI-SCOPE to detect another trigger. Refer to
                TRIGGER_HOLDOFF for more information.
            delay (float): How long the digitizer waits after receiving the trigger to start
                acquiring data. Refer to TRIGGER_DELAY_TIME for more
                information.
        '''
        if type(slope) is not enums.TriggerSlope:
            raise TypeError('Parameter mode must be of type ' + str(enums.TriggerSlope))
        vi_ctype = visatype.ViSession(self._vi)  # case 1

        error_code = self._library.niScope_ConfigureTriggerEdge(vi_ctype, trigger_source_ctype, level_ctype, slope_ctype, trigger_coupling_ctype, holdoff_ctype, delay_ctype)
        errors.handle_error(self, error_code, ignore_warnings=False, is_error_handling=False)
        return

issue_title: Create fake driver for test purposes
issue_body: We should provide fake driver metadata (NI-FAKE) that includes one instance of each API option we need to work with. issue_title: WebSocket opens, then immediately closes, than opens again and stays open, but only with wss
issue_body:
We have some websocket protocols on twisted and autobahn that we started to port to wss, and we stumble upon some odd behavior. When a websocket connect is open from chrome to our server, it initializes the protocol object, than immediately after that the connection is closed and reopened, and then it stays open. This is completely transparent on the browser, it can only be observed in the server logs. Bellow is the code that reproduces this in the latest version of both twisted and autobahn:

```
from datetime import datetime

from OpenSSL import crypto
from twisted.web import resource, server
from twisted.internet.task import react
from twisted.internet.defer import DeferredList, Deferred
from twisted.internet.ssl import CertificateOptions, Certificate, KeyPair
from twisted.internet.endpoints import SSL4ServerEndpoint
from autobahn.twisted.websocket import WebSocketServerProtocol, WebSocketServerFactory
import txaio


txaio.use_twisted()


CERT_KEY = "server.key"
CERT_PATH = "server.crt"


def log(msg):
    print("{}: {}".format(str(datetime.now()), msg))


class TestProtocol(WebSocketServerProtocol):
    """Dummy websocket protocol"""
    def __init__(self):
        super(TestProtocol, self).__init__()
        log("Test protocol init")

    def onMessage(self, payload, isBinary):
        log(payload)

    def connectionLost(self, reason):
        WebSocketServerProtocol.connectionLost(self, reason)
        log("Connection closed: Reason is {}".format(reason))


class TestProtocolFactory(WebSocketServerFactory):
    protocol = TestProtocol


class HTTPHandler(resource.Resource):
    """Dummy http handler, just a more handy way of accepting the self signed certificate"""
    isLeaf = True

    def render_GET(self, request):
        log("{}: {}".format(str(self), str(request)))
        return "Ok"


class HTTPProtocolFactory(object):
    """Not a real twisted factory, just to have the same interface on setup_protocols"""
    handler = HTTPHandler

    def __new__(cls):
        return server.Site(cls.handler())


def load_certificate(cert_key, cert_path):
    with open(cert_key) as key_file, open(cert_path) as cert_file:
        key = KeyPair.load(key_file.read(), crypto.FILETYPE_PEM).original
        cert = Certificate.loadPEM(cert_file.read()).original

    return CertificateOptions(
        privateKey=key,
        certificate=cert,
    )


def make_ssl_endpoint(reactor, port):
    cert_opts = load_certificate(CERT_KEY, CERT_PATH)
    return SSL4ServerEndpoint(reactor, port, cert_opts)


def setup_protocols(reactor):
    factories = {
        TestProtocolFactory: 9000,
        HTTPProtocolFactory: 8000
    }

    for factory, port in factories.items():
        endpoint = make_ssl_endpoint(reactor, port)
        endpoint.listen(factory())
        log("Bound port {} to protocol {}".format(str(port), factory))
    
    return Deferred()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    react(setup_protocols)
```

To reproduce, run the code above, go to chrome, access the https url, and accept the certificate (I'm using the one from the examples folder in this same repository). Then open the developers tools console and create a new websocket, like this: `var ws = new WebSocket("wss://localhost:9000");`. On the logs in the server, you should see the protocol being initialized, closed, then initialized again.

I google around and couldn't find anything similar, also posted a question on [stackoverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/39127928/twisted-autobahn-websocket-being-initialized-twice-with-wss), but even after updating to the recommending API, as the answer suggested, it still happening.

I tested this both with the versions we use in production, `Twisted==15.2.1` and `autobahn==0.10.5` and with the latest ones,  `Twisted==16.4.0` and `autobahn==0.16.0`, on a new virtualenv, and it happens on both versions. Both tests where on Ubuntu 14.04. Bellow is the output of `pip freeze` on the virtualenv where I tested with the latest versions:

```
attrs==16.0.0
autobahn==0.16.0
cffi==1.7.0
cryptography==1.5
enum34==1.1.6
idna==2.1
ipaddress==1.0.16
pyasn1==0.1.9
pyasn1-modules==0.0.8
pycparser==2.14
pyOpenSSL==16.1.0
service-identity==16.0.0
six==1.10.0
Twisted==16.4.0
txaio==2.5.1
wheel==0.26.0

issue_title: Best practices for token normalization with ELMo
issue_body:
I am trying to incorporate ELMo into my model, but I have a question about the best way to preprocess my input data.

Should I lowercase the tokens? I generally do this as standard practice, but I ran into an issue with beginning/end of sentence tokens. According to the [ELMo readme](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/tutorials/how_to/elmo.md), I need to add `<S>` and `</S>` to my input sentences. If I use allennlp's tokenizer to add these tokens and use the token indexer to lowercase the tokens, the beginning/end of sentence tokens are converted to `<s>` and `</s>`, which I assume means ELMo will not recognize them. If this is the case, could functionality be added to the token indexers to ignore normalizing specific tokens?

If I should not lowercase the input, then I think I will run into problems with using some pretrained word embeddings. The ELMo readme shows concatenating ELMo vectors to pretrained GloVe embeddings, which are already lowercased as far as I can tell. If I don't lowercase the input, then fewer of the tokens will have pretrained embeddings. I want to restrict my vocabulary to be only words which have pretrained embeddings, so this reduces the size of my vocabulary.

It would be great if the best practices for handling token normalization for ELMo could be added to the ELMo readme, especially because the details are pretty nuanced and hard to get right.

issue_title: Check that we have PyAV builds for the latest numpy
Incosistent imread() function behavior
issue_body:
Hello,

In [`image_sequence.py`](https://github.com/soft-matter/pims/blob/155b01dca6941c4d56ee8f8b075f788ef092b2f5/pims/image_sequence.py#L24-L33) there is a conditional import statement, in which an `imread()` function is imported from either scikit-image, matplotlib, or scipy. However, the behavior of each `imread()` function is different:

* `skimage.io.imread` does not normalize pixel values, so they are integers from 0-255
* `matplotlib.pyplot.imread` does normalize pixel values, so they are floats from 0-1
* `scipy.ndimage.imread` is deprecated in favor of `matplotlib.pyplot.imread` (as printed in the output of calls to scipy's `imread()` function), but it uses integer pixel values 0-255

In issue #121 there was discussion of whether to make scikit-image a dependency, and it sounds like the consensus is no. In light of this, because we cannot count on scikit-image being installed on a user's machine, and because matplotlib is a more lightweight requirement (in keeping with the philosophy of keeping pims lightweight), it might be best to change 24-33 to simply be:

```
from matplotlib.pyplot import imread
```

This would affect the output of any script that created an `ImageSequence` object and had `scikit-image` installed (for example, the walkthrough notebooks of trackpy, see issues soft-matter/trackpy-examples#43 and soft-matter/trackpy#455) so it is a potentially breaking change if users are counting on pixel values to be between 0-255. But I believe consistent, predictable behavior across machines is important.

Thoughts?

issue_title: In get_source() use mkstemp instead of  tmpfile = os.path.join(tmpdir, str(uuid.uuid4()))
Replace `delphin select` with tsql queries (maybe)
Automatically detect (TDL) file encoding from first line
issue_body:
TDL files seem to follow the emacs convention of having the first line specify the encoding, e.g.:

```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
```

But often there's this odd string:

```
;;; Hey, emacs(1), this is -*- Mode: TDL; Coding: utf-8; -*- got it?
```

Other settings on this line can probably be ignored, such as `Mode: TDL` and `indent-tabs-mode: nil`.

This may be useful for non-TDL files as well, so it could be implemented in `delphin.util`. Here's some pseudocode:

```python
def open_with_detected_encoding(filename, default_encoding='utf-8', comment_char=';'):
    # Open filename in binary mode and read one line
    # If the line has a comment_char and "coding:", extract the encoding
    # If the encoding is valid (e.g., using codecs.lookup()) then use it
    # Otherwise read and check the second line for "coding:" and use it if it's valid
    # Otherwise use the default encoding
    # Close filename
    # Open the filename (again), now in text mode with the selected encoding
    # Return the open file object
```

Also see here: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0263/#defining-the-encoding

Also make sure the `default_encoding` doesn't override UTF-8 if there is a UTF-8 BOM.

issue_title: conda install does unnecessary downgrade
issue_body:
```
$ conda --version
conda 3.17.0
```

Start with a Python 3.4 & NumPy 1.9 enviroment:
```
$ conda create -n foo python=3.4 numpy=1.9
...
The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    libgfortran: 1.0-0         defaults
    numpy:       1.9.2-py34_1  defaults
    openblas:    0.2.14-1      defaults
    openssl:     1.0.1k-1      defaults
    pip:         7.1.2-py34_0  defaults
    python:      3.4.3-1       defaults
    readline:    6.2-2         defaults
    setuptools:  18.1-py34_0   defaults
    sqlite:      3.8.4.1-1     defaults
    system:      5.8-2         defaults
    tk:          8.5.18-0      defaults
    wheel:       0.24.0-py34_0 defaults
    xz:          5.0.5-0       defaults
    zlib:        1.2.8-0       defaults
```

Attempting to add a package that has been built for Python 3.4 and both NumPy 1.8 and 1.9 results in a seemingly erroneous attempt to downgrade NumPy:
```
$ conda install -n foo -c scitools mo_pack
...
The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    libmo_unpack: 3.0-1            scitools
    mo_pack:      0.2.0-np18py34_1 scitools

The following packages will be DOWNGRADED:

    numpy:        1.9.2-py34_1 defaults --> 1.8.2-py34_0     defaults
```

Adding the package with an explicit NumPy version demonstrates that a suitable distribution is available as it avoids the downgrade:
```
$ conda install -n foo -c scitools mo_pack numpy=1.9
...
The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    libmo_unpack: 3.0-1            scitools
    mo_pack:      0.2.0-np19py34_1 scitools
```

issue_title: conda search --outdated doesn't take into account the build number
default to channel-priority = False
issue_body:
The current (new) default changes the old behaviour of conda which means that a lot of users will be surprised when they update to the new conda versions (I certainly was :-/).

IMO it's also a more sane behaviour: currently the priorities mean that newer versions gets downgraded on updates which is not what I want when I use both conda-forge and defaults. 

Its also potentially harmfull as new new channels are added as the highest priority and so this channel can bring back old packages: with the old conda versions and a user adding a channel for "just one newer package", old leftover packages are not used -> New channels were only harmful when the channel owner explicitly would add a harmful package with a newer version number but now a not updated test package can happen to be installed.

It would also be nice if the help would tell one how to influence channel priority.

The current option is also advertised as beta and subject to change which is IMO an argument to not make it active on default.

```
 --channel-priority, --channel-pri, --chan-pri
                        Channel priority takes precedence over packaage
                        version (default: True). Note: This feature is in beta
                        and may change in a future release.
```

[`packaage` -> `package`?]

issue_title: VariantRecord.items() segfaults on missing genotypes
issue_body:
Iteration through sample records of a `VariantFile` throws a segfault (code 11) when any sample has missing genotypes *and* an attempt is made to fetch genotypes for that sample or any other.  Iteration over the `VariantRecordSample` entries appears to be intact as long as the genotypes are not processed.

A minimal example follows.  First the test VCF file (`test.vcf`):
```
##fileformat=VCFv4.1
##contig=<ID=chr1,length=100>
##FORMAT=<ID=GT,Number=1,Type=String,Description="Genotype">
#CHROM	POS	ID	REF	ALT	QUAL	FILTER	INFO	FORMAT	sample1	sample2
chr1	1	.	A	G	999	.	.	GT	0/0	1/1
chr1	50	.	T	C	999	.	.	GT	./.	0/1
```

And now console output
```

issue_title: Allow Repository Validation to be disabled
issue_body:
Currently, when a snapshot is used, the repository verification API is invoked. If there's a hiccup in this check, then it prevents the snapshot. It's a pretty reasonable check to make and it should certainly be default, but sometimes the failure is worth trying to go around (particularly with debugging).

```
curator snapshot --skip-repo-validation
```

issue_title: [Docs] Document the date math method for each time unit
issue_body:
Because of the way curator 4.x calculates time in the various units, it would be good to document how each of these unites are derived, as not all calculations will necessarily align with calendar-based math.

As outlined here: https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/utils.py#L232-L265

For example, a month is assumed to be 30 days, rather than however many days are in a given calendar month. So documenting this would be great, for the sake of understanding and planning for this behavior.

issue_title: Address API change when array of aliases used
issue_body:
https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/5.5/breaking-changes-5.5.html#breaking_55_rest_changes

> Previous versions of Elasticsearch did not return a 404 in all cases when a requested alias or a requested type did not exist on the get alias or get mapping APIs, respectively. Starting in Elasticsearch 5.5.0, these APIs consistently return a 404 in any case when an alias or type is requested that does not exist.

What this means is that if you provide a list of aliases to filter on, if the index is not in ALL indices, you will get a 404.  This caused a test which passed in ES 5.4 to fail in ES 5.5:
```
======================================================================
FAIL: test_filter_by_array_of_aliases (test.integration.test_integrations.TestFilters)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/buh/Dropbox/Development/curator/test/integration/test_integrations.py", line 55, in test_filter_by_array_of_aliases
    self.assertEquals(1, len(curator.get_indices(self.client)))
AssertionError: 1 != 2
```
and the test looks like this:
```
    def test_filter_by_array_of_aliases(self):
        alias = 'testalias'
        self.write_config(
            self.args['configfile'], testvars.client_config.format(host, port))
        self.write_config(self.args['actionfile'],
            testvars.filter_by_alias.format(' [ testalias, foo ]', False))
        self.create_index('my_index')
        self.create_index('dummy')
        self.client.indices.put_alias(index='dummy', name=alias)
        test = clicktest.CliRunner()
        result = test.invoke(
                    curator.cli,
                    [
                        '--config', self.args['configfile'],
                        self.args['actionfile']
                    ],
                    )
        self.assertEquals(1, len(curator.get_indices(self.client)))
```
emphasis on `testvars.filter_by_alias.format(' [ testalias, foo ]', False))`

issue_title: Add .watches to kibana filter
issue_body: Presently, the kibana filtertype removes "indices .kibana, .marvel-kibana, kibana-int, and .marvel-es-data from the list of indices", according to https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/filtertype_kibana.html . For people using Watcher, I think they'd be sad if their .watches index was accidentially gone / they'd be grateful if this filter saved it.

--Manoj

issue_title: [Documentation] Please document upgrade procedures
issue_body:
Please document the best way to upgrade to the latest release when not using apt-get (e.g, on Windows)
Do I re-run 'pip install --user azure-cli'?  Or do I run 'az component update' with no other parameters?

issue_title: az eventhubs namespace create shows wrong help
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**

`az eventhubs namespace create --help`  shows this as an example. 

```
        az eventhubs namespace create --resource-group myresourcegroup --name mynamespace --location
        westus --tags tag1=value1 tag2=value2 --sku-name Standard --sku-tier Standard --is-auto-
        inflate-enabled False --maximum-throughput-units 30
```

I'm not sure which is correct. However `--sku Standard` might be right.

issue_title: Unable to get kube/.config with multiple masters
issue_body:
Hi,

when I run the following:
```
az group create --name=$RESOURCE_GROUP --location=$LOCATION

az acs create --orchestrator-type=kubernetes --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP --name=$CLUSTER_NAME --dns-prefix=$DNS_PREFIX --agent-count=$NUM_AGENTS --ssh-key-value=$SSH_PUB_KEY

az acs kubernetes get-credentials --resource-group=$RESOURCE_GROUP --name=$CLUSTER_NAME --ssh-key-file=$SSH_PRIV_KEY
```

`get-credentials` fails with: `scp: .kube/config: No such file or directory`

I tried to ssh into one of the master nodes and ~/.kube/config didn't exist. Everything was working for me when I tried a couple weeks ago and I think I tracked the issue down to PR #2210. Indeed when I create a single master cluster:
```
az acs create --orchestrator-type=kubernetes --resource-group $RESOURCE_GROUP --name=$CLUSTER_NAME --dns-prefix=$DNS_PREFIX --agent-count=$NUM_AGENTS --ssh-key-value=$SSH_PUB_KEY --master-count 1
```
A subsequent `az acs kubernetes get-credentials` call succeeds.

Briefly, I am on macOS and `az --version` shows that I am on azure-cli 2.0.0.

issue_title: 'az account set' does not work
issue_body:
# 'az account set' does not work on macOs Sierra 10.12.3
install latest version Azure CLI 2.0
```
bash-3.2$ az --version
azure-cli (2.0.1)
```
Do not install Azure CLI 1, i.e. `azure` command must not be available
login with 
`bash-3.2$ az login`
Let's say you have two accounts/subscriptions:
`bash-3.2$ az account list`
```
[
  {
    "cloudName": "AzureCloud",
    "isDefault": true,
    "name": "Name 1",
    "registeredProviders": [],
    "state": "Enabled",
    "tenantId": "374f8....d",
    "user": {
      "name": "user.name@company.com",
      "type": "user"
    }
  },
  {
    "cloudName": "AzureCloud",
    "id": "4...e2",
    "isDefault": false,
    "name": "Name 2",
    "registeredProviders": [],
    "state": "Enabled",
    "tenantId": "3...d",
    "user": {
      "name": "user.name@company.com",
      "type": "user"
    }
  }
]
```
try to perform any command,e.g. 
`bash-3.2$az vm list -g 'Name 2'`
it fails with 

> please run 'az account set' to select active account

run 
`bash-3.2$ az account set --subscription 'Name 2'`
it exits silently
repeat original command, it still fails with the same error message
`bash-3.2$az vm list -g 'Name 2'`
`please run 'az account set' to select active account`

> 

UPDATE: installing Azure CLI 1.0 and logging with it
`azure login`
fixes the issue

issue_title: Job template wont create task if i add resource files collection
Captizalization
issue_body:
Some words follow special capitalization rules.  For instance, prepositions should be lowercase, and roman numeral all caps ("Rua do Arvoredo", "Rua João XXIII").

issue_title: WebSocketResponse does not throw/close when connection is abruptly cut
issue_body:
## Long story short

This is a continuation of #961 (with some ties to #2283). Apparently, the issue is still valid (I don't know if it was ever really fixed).

I have a single websocket handler. It does two things on connection:

* Starts a sender task that sends `N` bytes over the websocket every second
* Receives (and discards) any incoming websocket messages

I connect to this handler through a simple javascript client running in Google Chrome. Everything works as expected: I receive `N` bytes every second, indefinitely.

However, when the connection is abruptly cut (by disconnecting the network cable), the websocket sender task takes 15 minutes to stop (even though the heartbeat timeout is only 5 seconds).

## Expected behaviour

I would expect the `send_bytes` operation to throw *or* the `async for msg in ws` loop to terminate/throw.

## Actual behaviour

The `send_bytes` keep buffering up writes (eventually causing the process to run out of memory and crash). Furthermore, the `async for msg in ws` loop takes 15 minutes to terminate/throw.

## Steps to reproduce

The websocket server:

	#!/usr/bin/env python3.6
	import aiohttp
	import asyncio
	import logging
	from aiohttp import web

	logger = logging.getLogger()
	logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

	async def sender(ws):
		try:
			logging.info('sender begin')
			while True:
				# Sender never stops on disconnect for N = 10000
				N = 100000
				# Sender stops on disconnect for N = 10
				#N = 10
				logging.info(f'sending {N} bytes')
				try:
					ws.send_bytes(bytes(N))

					# Explicit drain will stall the sender on disconnect
					# but not throw (despite of the heartbeat)
					#await ws.drain()

					# The only fix is to query transport.is_closing()
					# This stops the transport as soon as the heartbeat times out
					#if ws._req.transport.is_closing():
					#	logging.info(f'sender transport is closing')
					#	break
				except Exception as error:
					logging.info(f'sender error:', exc_info=error)
				await asyncio.sleep(1.0)
		# Catch all sender Exceptions
		except Exception as error:
			logging.info('sender error:', type(error))
		finally:
			logging.info('sender end')

	async def handler(request):
		# Note the hearbeat is set to 5 seconds
		ws = web.WebSocketResponse(heartbeat=5.0)
		await ws.prepare(request)

		# Launch send task
		sender_task = asyncio.ensure_future(sender(ws))

		# Pass any message
		logging.info('receiver begin')
		try:
			async for msg in ws:
				pass
		# Catch all receiver Exceptions
		except Exception as error:
			logging.info(f'receiver error:', exc_info=error)
		finally:
			logging.info('receiver end')

		# Cancel sender task
		sender_task.cancel()

		return ws

	app = web.Application()
	app.router.add_route('GET', '/', handler)
	web.run_app(app, port=5006)

The javascript client (wrapped in HTML for browser use):

	<html>
	    <script>
	        ws = new WebSocket('ws://192.168.1.111:5006/');
	        ws.onmessage = (message) => console.log(message);
	        ws.onerror = (error) => console.error(error);
	        ws.onclose = () => console.log('closed');
	    </script>
	</html>

Note that this only happens for large `N` (around 10000). Everything works fine for say `N = 10`. I don't know why. Maybe some internal caching/buffering is causing this issue?

Note that using `await ws.drain()` only stalls the sender. This way, the process doesn't run out of memory but the connection is still in limbo.

The practical fix is to query ` ws._req.transport.is_closing()` (as pointed out by ttm56p in #2283).

I think that aiohttp should handle this internally by whatever means necessary.

It took a long time for me to track down a memory leak to this issue. The writes just kept buffering up. Note that this also happens if you simply let a browser tab sit idle for a long time (apparently, Chrome will stop sending websocket data after a period of inactivity in the tab). In practice, I saw my websocket servers crashing every now and then.

## Your environment

Aiohttp 2.2.3 and Python 3.6.1. Google Chrome 61.0.

            await cur.execute("INSERT INTO test VALUES (1)")

loop.run_until_complete(test_example())

issue_title: Always use keyword-only style for optional arguments
issue_body:
Let's assume we have a public API function with the following signature: `def f(arg, flag1=None, flag2=True, flag3=False)`.
User is supposed to call it as `f('arg', flag2=False)`.
But now it might do it as `f('arg', None, False)` which makes a mess.

I suggest changing all such signatures to `def f(arg, *, flag1=None, flag2=True, flag3=False)`.
It forces user to specify optional args by name only.

@achimnol ?

issue_title: Add cursor movement within words to next underscore
Doc build fails if IPython is installed system-wide
issue_body:
Hi, I'm trying to update the Fedora RPM package to 5.0.0. Part of the package build process is rebuilding the docs.

When the documentation of notebook-5.0.0 is built, the notebook file [docs/source/examples/Notebook/Importing Notebooks.ipynb](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/5.0.0/docs/source/examples/Notebook/Importing%20Notebooks.ipynb) is evaluated and the following code is run:

```python
import shutil
from IPython.paths import get_ipython_package_dir

utils = os.path.join(get_ipython_package_dir(), 'utils')
shutil.copy(os.path.join("nbpackage", "mynotebook.ipynb"),
            os.path.join(utils, "inside_ipython.ipynb")
)
```

This of course fails when run under non-root user and IPython is installed system-wide:

```
PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: '/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/IPython/utils/inside_ipython.ipynb'

You can ignore this error by setting the following in conf.py:

    nbsphinx_allow_errors = True
```

As a workaround I can use suggested `nbsphinx_allow_errors = True`, but that may be dangerous as it is set globally, not just for this one file. I'm not yet sure what it does exactly, but the docs might end up rendered with error, so I's rather not use it at all.

What is a benefit of this particular example (putting a notebook inside an existing, already installed module)? Can this section be removed form that example file? (I'll probably end up patching it out in Fedora, as it would never work for regular user anyway, but would rather make it an upstream change.) Thanks.

issue_title: Vertical and horizontal scrollbar not both visible in toggled output
issue_body:
When displaying a large pandas DataFrame, you get some scrollbars. And starting from pandas 0.16.2, we leave the overflowing fully to the notebook itself (we removed `style="max-width:1500px;overflow:auto;"` from the html output, see https://github.com/pydata/pandas/pull/10232).

But I noticed another issue: when having a wide frame that does not fit the notebook width, you get horizontal scrollbar at the bottom of the output. When you also use the 'scroll output' feature, you get a vertical scrollbar as well. But the horizontal is only visible when you first scroll down to the bottom. To illustrate:

![nb2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1020496/9037638/97ebdac0-39ed-11e5-8a04-fae8927bded9.png)

![nb1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1020496/9037639/9a86b5fc-39ed-11e5-89c5-57865b47760f.png)

So if you want to see all columns of the first rows, you first have to scroll down, to be able to scroll right, then scroll up again to see the rows.

I suppose the one scrollbar belongs to the output itself, and the other to the toggled ouput area, but would it be possible to have them both on the toggled output area?

issue_title: np.linalg.norm gradient issue
issue_body:
Hi, 

I was getting nans at zeroafter evaluating the gradient of a function that was using np.linalg.norm. Couldn't figure it out. After an embarrassingly long time doing other checks, I tried replacing it with a np.sum expression which seemed to work fine. Just wanted to bring it to your attention. 

Thanks, 
Arjumand

Sample Code: 
```import autograd.numpy as np 
import autograd as au 

def f_sum(x):
    return np.exp(-1.0*np.sum((x**2)))

def f_norm(x):
    return np.exp(-1.0*np.linalg.norm(x)**2)

df_sum = au.grad(f_sum)
df_norm = au.grad(f_norm)

x = np.zeros((2,1))

print "f:", f_sum(x), f_norm(x)
#prints 1.0 1.0
print "using sum:"
print df_sum(x) 
#prints [0,0]
print "using norm:"
print df_norm(x)
#prints [nan, nan]
```

issue_title: a less implementation-dependent test set
issue_body:
It would be nice to be able to specify a test like this:

- Initial files.
- Series of commands.
- Expected output.

The series of commands would probably include the ability to invoke random Python scripts checked in with the test, so you could do stuff like write or delete files in the middle, without needing to specify some kludgy mini-language.

If there were multiple implementations of peru, this could give us a shareable set of tests. On the downside, it would be harder to test random flags or check for specific error types. Probably not an idea worth implementing, but I'm writing this issue just to think about it out loud.

issue_title: Enable parallel build if support by underlying build tool
issue_body:
- If Makefile Unix, Ninja or Jom based generator are used:  `-j<number_of_core>`
- If Visual Studio:
  - `/MP<number_of_core>` 
  - `/maxcpucount:<number_of_core>` 

Note that for complex project, it could be hard to find the right setting for `/MP` and `/maxcpucount`. For example, you want to avoid too many link steps requiring a lot of memory to happen at the same time ....  Having a way to disable parallel build may be a sensible option.

More details:
- With VS >= 2010, target level parallelism can be set from command line using  `/maxcpucount`. (Number of simultaneous MSBuild.exe processes). See [1]
- object level parallelism set using `/MP`   (Number of simultaneous cl.exe processes). See [2]

[1] https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/bb651793%28v=vs.120%29.aspx
[2] https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/bb385193.aspx

Here is some more details of what we did for Slicer: http://massmail.spl.harvard.edu/public-archives/slicer-devel/2016/019656.html

issue_title: API addition to get current key(s) for verifying ID-linked signatures
issue_body:
**What we're building:**

We are working on a service that dispatches a payload for signing to one or multiple users. These users then sign the payload with whatever key is associated with their blockchain-anchored ID. The service needs to verify the signed payload when it receives it back to ensure it is signed buy the user ID that was intended.

**What we need:**

We would like a simple API method that returns the latest public key(s) a user has associated with their ID.

issue_title: Add nominal support for KMIP 1.4
issue_body:
While PyKMIP does not support any specific KMIP 1.4 features, the message formats are still compatible. Currently the server will reject KMIP 1.4 request messages simply based on the fact that 1.4 is not a supported version number. This means that 1.4 Create, Get, Destroy, etc requests will fail when they shouldn't.

Bump the KMIP version number supported by the server to "officially" support KMIP 1.4.

issue_title: Dependency in __init__ means pip install fails
issue_body:
pyparsing is a dependency for bibtexparser and is noted as such in setup.py.

**However**, setup.py gets the version number from __init__, which in turn imports bibtexexpression, which imports pyparsing.

As a result, in an install where pyparsing is not already installed, attempting to install via pip will result in an error:

    $ cd /path/to/project
    $ virtualenv .
    $ source ./bin/activate
    $ pip install -e git+https://github.com/sciunto-org/python-bibtexparser.git#egg=bibtexparser
    Obtaining bibtexparser from git+https://github.com/sciunto-org/python-bibtexparser.git#egg=bibtexparser
      Updating ./src/bibtexparser clone
        Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
        Traceback (most recent call last):
          File "<string>", line 20, in <module>
          File "/path/to/project/src/bibtexparser/setup.py", line 8, in <module>
            from bibtexparser import __version__ as version
          File "bibtexparser/__init__.py", line 27, in <module>
            from . import bibdatabase, bibtexexpression, bparser, bwriter, latexenc, customization
          File "bibtexparser/bibtexexpression.py", line 1, in <module>
            import pyparsing as pp
        ImportError: No module named pyparsing

issue_title: Proposal: Use decorator instead of get_classes to mark classes extensible
issue_labels: [{'id': 89933, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-oscar/django-oscar/labels/%E2%98%85%20New%20feature', 'name': '★ New feature', 'color': '9FE9A5', 'default': False}, {'id': 23923305, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-oscar/django-oscar/labels/%E2%9A%9B%20Research', 'name': '⚛ Research', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False}, {'id': 19509306, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-oscar/django-oscar/labels/%E2%9C%8D%20For%20review', 'name': '✍ For review', 'color': '009800', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
While struggling with the pain that is `get_classes` statements spanning across multiple lines, I wished for a cleaner way to handle Oscar's dynamic class loading
### Proposal

Introduce a class decorator that mimics `get_class`. If a customised version of the decorated class exists, return that one instead of the decorated class.

```
# catalogue/forms.py
from oscar.core.loading import extensible

class ProductForm(forms.Form):
    ...

@extensible
class ExtensibleProductForm(ProductForm):
    pass

# catalogue/views.py

from .forms import ExtensibleProductForm
```
### Pro & Con

Pro:
- more readable import statements
- IDE support for importing, and detecting unused or wrong imports
- avoids accidentally using a direct import, class will always be extensible
- a lot DRYer than the current solution

Cons:
- It's not really "decorating", it's a straight-out monkey patch.
- Makes class names longer

One could also just decorate all extensible classes, but then it's not immediately obvious from other code if the used class is extensible or not. And it makes it harder to access the core Oscar class.

issue_title: Confusing naming and handling of certificates and configuration, leading to unpredictable client behavior
issue_body:
I am quite confused by how letsencrypt decides to name certificates when a certificate covers multiple domains, and confused about the relative scope of the global `cli.conf` and the files in `renew`.
By the "name" of a certificate, I mean the folder below `/etc/letsencrypt/{live,archive}` that the certificate is stored in. It seems to be the case that letsencrypt picks the first or the last domain name specified for this purpose. However, I couldn't see this properly documented (definitely not in `--help`). Since this name will probably be used throughout the lifetime of the server (or is there a way to rename?), this is rather annoying - I ended up with `randomsubdomain.mydomain.de` as the name. It is also entirely unclear what happens when I add more domains to the beginning / end of the list of current domains. Will it again pick the first domain, which is now a different one, and hence create a new cert that is entirely unrelated to the old one? Or will it somehow notice that some of the domains are already in an existing cert, and extend that? There could easily be multiple candidates though.

I *thought* that the files in `/etc/letsencrypt/renew/*.conf` would help me understand what is happening, but these files do not seem to change even when a certificate is successfully updated (e.g., when new domains are added). So now I don't even know whether changes in the `cli.conf` will affect any particular action I perform, since old copies of that configuration are stored in `renew` and may or may not be used whenever I do anything. The folder name seems to indictae that it is only used for automatic renewal, but then why should "get me a new cert and add this domain" be fundamentally different from "get me a new cert and keep the existing set of domains"? Really, renewal and changes in configuration are almost the same process, so I would think they also behave almost the same. or, put differently, changes in configuration can be decomposed into "change the target-state of the certificate", and "make a new certificate with the current target state", whereas renewal is just the second part.

All this ended up being so confusing and unpredictable, that I ended up running into the per-domain rate-limiting for one of my domains before I even understood what the client is doing. For my next domain, I ended up using a different client, that is way less convenient - but has predictable behavior, even without reading any docs.

One of the core problems here is that "one of the domains the cert is valid for" is just the wrong kind of ID to name a sequence of cert-key-pairs that are considered successors of each other: The set of domains that are covered by the cert may change at any time. As time goes on, the domains that were originally in the cert could *all* be gone, and new domains be added, but the entire chain of cert-key-pairs can still be considered as belonging together. At any given point in time, no single domain may be the "main" domain, and even if there is a "main" one, it may change.
From what I understand, letsencrypt is managing a set of such "cert-key-sequences". I can modify existing such sequences, extend them with a new cert-key pair, keep the latest private key but get a new cert, change their configuration (set of domains, key size, whatever). I think this is a reasonable simple mental model, and everything letsencrypt does could be explained in terms of it. But the UI reflects this very poorly, IMHO, making it hard to map calls to letsencrypt, and changes in the global `cli.conf`, to this model.

I suggest that letsencrypt makes a consistent distinction between a *certificate sequence* (there may be a better name for this) with some arbitrarily user-picked name for it, and the (set of) *domain*s the latest certificate of a sequence is valid for. It should always be clear whether an action creates an entirely new cert-seq or whether an existing cert-seq is extended. When a cert-seq already exists, it should always be clear whether a new private key is generated, or whether the previously existing private key is re-used.
I thought of something like this: All letsencrypt invocations can take a `-n/--name` parameter, which means they affect only the cert-seq stored in `/etc/letsencrypt/{live,archive}/<name>`. Some place is used to store interesting configuration for this sequence, e.g. `renew/<name>.conf`. When working on an existing sequence, one can use `-d` to add a new domain to the next certificate that wasn't present in the previous one, and some other flag to remove a domain. The file in `renew/` always stores the current configuration of a sequence (the folder name doesn't really work then Any more, though). This includes exact current set of domains (matching the SANs in the certificate), and potentially more if the per-cert configuration differs from the global one (which can happen if the user sets flags on the CLI). That information is enough to do automatic renewal, but it is also used when e.g. adding a new domain to a sequence - all the other configuration stays the same. All the remaining configuration should either fall back to the global `cli.conf`, or it must be entirely clear how changes in the global configuration affect existing certificate sequences, and how to change the per-sequence config.

Or maybe this is entirely overkill, and there already is an easy mental model for what letsencrypt does right now, matching its UI. But then I would argue that model is not intuitively understood from the `--help` output, the name of the parameters, and the file structure stored in `/etc/letsencrypt`.
You probably don't want to change how everything works just for the fun of it, and I'm just talking here while you are doing all the work. That said, I'm very willing to work on improving this myself - hopefully I can find some of you guys at 32C3 :)

issue_title: Release 0.0.0.dev20151008
Directory name "/.well-known" seems to be reserved on OS X Server, making challenge verification impossible
issue_body:
During the `letsencrypt` authentication process, I must put a challenge response on my server, at the URL path:

`http://example.com/.well-known/acme-challenge/<challenge key>`

However, on my OS X Server, while most dot-hidden paths like `/.the-directory` are served just fine, I seem to have narrowed down that `/.well-known`, in specific, always returns a 503 error message, even while OS X Server's Web services are turned on and reponding otherwise normally:

> Websites are turned off.  
> An administrator can turn them on using the Server application.

I thought I might be able to rewrite or redirect from `/.well-known` to a different directory on my server, but having tried both of those tacks via .htaccess overrides, as well as with the OS X Server GUI Aliases and Redirects settings, nothing I do will seem to make the server respond to an URL including `/.well-known`, despite being able to alias and redirect any other directory name that I could think of to test.

A cursory look at OS X Server's `httpd_server_app.conf` and related files didn't reveal anything, but it would seem that by default `/.well-known` may be reserved by the system for some reason. Is there any way to resolve this conflict? The `letsencrypt` client seems to be hard wired to look for the challenge answer at this path, but is it possible to configure letsencrypt to check an alternate path?

issue_title: Unhandled exceptions on json.loads give no clues on connection errors to Adyen
issue_body:
**Python version**: 2.7.12
**Library version**: 1.1.0, 1.2.0, development
**Description**

Sometimes we have big periods of time that we get following error from Adyen client:
```
ValueError('No JSON could be decoded')
``` 
To make the problem worse this exception tell us nothing about what we are sending and receiving to Adyen servers, and so we can't investigate the problem because we have no clues about it.

We need that devs handle the `json_lib.loads` call at https://github.com/Adyen/adyen-python-api-library/blob/1.1.0/Adyen/client.py#L413 and instead `ValueError` return an `AdyenInvalidRequestError` with request information so we can investigate the problem.

Cheers!

issue_title: Reconnect on lost network interface
issue_body:
When loosing the network connection (e.g. on mobile devices when switching between different wifis or mobile data), using `client.reconnect()` does not work. I think one needs to drop and create a new socket in this case.

One workaround would be to periodically invoke some request (e.g. `GetStateRequest`). In case `TimeoutError` is raised, one can create a completely new instance of `TelegramClient`.

But I guess there already is some kind of ping between server and client, to test whether the connection is still alive? A better solution would be to have some subscribable event or callback which is triggered if this ping signal the connection is lost. Then each application could decide what to, e.g. wait and try reconnect or create a new client instance.

Also, some function like `client.is_connected()` would be nice.

issue_title: Support for HTTPS/SSL
issue_body:
I spent a bit of time trying to figure out why my map wasn't rendering inline in ipython notebook following these examples: http://nbviewer.ipython.org/gist/bburky/7763555/folium-ipython.ipynb. The problem is that I was running ipython notebook over https, and ssl isn't available for OpenStreetMap's public tiles.

Stamen makes SSL tiles freely available (http://maps.stamen.com/, ctrl-f ssl):

 > If you'd like to display these map tiles on a website that requires HTTPS, use our tile SSL endpoint by replacing http://tile.stamen.com with https://stamen-tiles.a.ssl.fastly.net. Multiple subdomains can be also be used: https://stamen-tiles-{S}.a.ssl.fastly.net
 > 
 > JavaScript can be loaded from https://stamen-maps.a.ssl.fastly.net/js/tile.stamen.js.

Perhaps using a protocol relative URL for stamen tiles, and making them default for SSL (or all the time-- they're gorgeous!), would make folium more easy to pick up, since some beginner ipython noteobok tutorials set up SSL.

A warning message in the documentation about the availability of certain tiles through SSL could be helpful as well.

issue_body:

```python
class DocSchema(Schema):
    str = fields.String()
   bool = fields.Boolean()

# Ok, None is not implicitly acceptable

issue_title: `exclude` in Meta breaks with marshmallow 3
issue_body:
We have a pattern where we exclude some relations from the marshmallow schema like this:

```python
class CustomerSchema(MyBaseSchema):
    class Meta(MyBaseSchema.Meta):
        model = Customer
        exclude = ('orders',)
```
Where `Customer` is a sqlalchemy model and `orders` is a relation from another table.

This works fine with marshmallow 2.x, but with marshmallow 3, an exception is thrown in the instantiation of `CustomerSchema` because marshmallow-sqlalchemy did not create the `orders` field, and so `orders` doesn't exist to be excluded.

See also https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/877

issue_title: _update_break_state is broken on mac due to wrong ioctl value
issue_body:
I tested this on a Macbook Pro the other day, opening a serial port and setting the break_condition property leads to an OS Error 

```
File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/serial/serialutil.py", line 479, in break_condition
    self._update_break_state()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/serial/serialposix.py", line 620, in _update_break_state
    fcntl.ioctl(self.fd, TIOCSBRK)
OSError: [Errno 25] Inappropriate ioctl for device
```

This is due to the linux values for TIOCSBRK and TIOCCBRK being used, instead of the mac (bsd kernel headers) values for those ioctl syscalls.

The correct values would need to be added to the library in order for this functionality to work

issue_body:
# Detection issue:
Multiple sends in a single transaction

## Description: 
External calls can fail accidentally or deliberately. Avoid combining multiple send() calls in a single transaction.

## Link
https://consensys.github.io/smart-contract-best-practices/recommendations/#favor-pull-over-push-for-external-calls

## Implementation details:
This can be easily done by looking for all call instructions, and checking if the cfg after the instruction contains more calls.

issue_title: Allow to choose certificate to use to avoid error "Could not find any matching SSL certificate"
issue_body:
When running 'senza create...', Senza asks which domain to use for the stack.
It then fails because there is no certificate matching that exact domain name.

Can Senza ask which certificate to use, if none matching the domain name is found?

Repro steps:

Create configuration (command from [documentation](http://stups.readthedocs.org/en/latest/components/senza.html)):
```
senza init myapp.yaml --region "eu-west-1"
Please select the project template
1) bgapp: Background app with single EC2 instance
2) postgresapp: HA Postgres app, which needs an S3 bucket to store WAL files
3) rediscluster: Elasticache cluster running multiple redis nodes, with replication / HA
4) redisnode: Elasticache node running redis, without replication / HA (for caching)
5) webapp: HTTP app with auto scaling, ELB and DNS
Please select (1-5) [5]:
Application ID [hello-world]:
Docker image without tag/version (e.g. "pierone.example.org/myteam/myapp") [stups/hello-world]:
HTTP port [8080]:
HTTP health check path [/]:
EC2 instance type [t2.micro]:
Did you need OAuth-Credentials from Mint? [y/N]:
Please select the load balancer scheme
1) internal: only accessible from the own VPC
2) internet-facing: accessible from the public internet
Please select (1-2) [1]:
Checking security group app-hello-world.. OK
Checking security group app-hello-world-lb.. OK
Checking IAM role app-hello-world.. OK
Generating Senza definition file myapp.yaml.. OK
```
Create stack from configuration (command from [documentation](http://stups.readthedocs.org/en/latest/components/senza.html)):
```
senza create myapp.yaml 1 0.1 --region "eu-west-1"
Generating Cloud Formation template..Please select the domain
1) mywebsite.at
2) mywebsite.be
3) mywebsite.ch
4) mywebsite.com
5) mywebsite.de
6) mywebsite.es
7) mywebsite.fr
8) mywebsite.it
Please select (1-8): 4
Could not find any matching SSL certificate for "mywebsite-com"
```

Note: in AWS, we do **not** have one certificate for every domain, we have the following certs (edited):
(from 'aws iam list-server-certificates':)
- mywebsite.at.2016 (used for mywebsite.at, mywebsite.fr)
- mywebsite.com.2016 (used for mywebsite.be, mywebsite.ch, mywebsite.com, mywebsite.de, mywebsite.es, mywebsite.it)

Is there a way to specify which certificate to use per domain?

issue_title: OCaml bindings for mk_fpa_max mk_fpa_min acting weird.
issue_body:
I use the following code to get the minimum of two numbers (in this case the same number twice).

```
let _ =
  try
    let ctx = Z3.mk_context [] in
    let ft = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_sort_32 ctx in 
    let t1 = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_numeral_f ctx 0.5 ft in
    let t2 = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_min ctx t1 t1 in
    let t3 = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_eq ctx t1 t2 in 
    let solver = Z3.Solver.mk_simple_solver ctx in
    let result = Z3.Solver.check solver [t3] in
    let s = 
      match result with
      | Z3.Solver.SATISFIABLE -> "sat"
      | Z3.Solver.UNSATISFIABLE -> "unsat"
      | Z3.Solver.UNKNOWN -> "unknown"
    in
    Printf.printf "Test Z3: The check yields: %s\n" s
  with
  | Z3native.Exception exn -> Printf.printf "Test Z3: Z3native.Exception: %s\n" exn
  | _ -> Printf.printf "Test Z3: failed\n"
```
Unfortunately Z3 reports unknown for this code. This can't be right, can it?

Another weird message pops up using the same code to get the max of two (identical) numbers:
```
let _ =
  try
    let ctx = Z3.mk_context [] in
    let ft = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_sort_32 ctx in 
    let t1 = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_numeral_f ctx 0.5 ft in
    let t2 = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_max ctx t1 t1 in
    let t3 = Z3.FloatingPoint.mk_eq ctx t1 t2 in 
    let solver = Z3.Solver.mk_simple_solver ctx in
    let result = Z3.Solver.check solver [t3] in
    let s = 
      match result with
      | Z3.Solver.SATISFIABLE -> "sat"
      | Z3.Solver.UNSATISFIABLE -> "unsat"
      | Z3.Solver.UNKNOWN -> "unknown"
    in
    Printf.printf "Test Z3: The check yields: %s\n" s
  with
  | Z3native.Exception exn -> Printf.printf "Test Z3: Z3native.Exception: %s\n" exn
  | _ -> Printf.printf "Test Z3: failed\n"
```
Now Z3 throws an Exception "Z3native.Exception: invalid argument".
          
I have no clue why min and max behave differently in the first place. 
Next thing is why unknown or an Exception pops up. 
All other tests I ran seemed to be fine 
(I have written tests for almost all the floating point operations).

I am running Debian (Linux version 3.16.0-4-amd64) and Z3 4.4.2 
(downloaded and compiled today without problems). 
I also tried with Z3 4.4.0 and got the same output.

Any suggestions that might help to solve this are appreciated.

issue_title: Potential data race in util/gparams.cpp
issue_body:
I found an insufficient synchronization that may cause data race in the following code.

https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/c64d044e60684aa54c8c4d5cc4d81460707bc65d/src/util/gparams.cpp#L440-L448

When the above function returns, the local variable result "params_ref result" will be destructed. Thus, if there are two threads calling the function and returning at the same time, the destructor of params_ref, i.e., ~params_ref(), will be called at the same time in the two threads. The code of the destructor is as following:

https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/c64d044e60684aa54c8c4d5cc4d81460707bc65d/src/util/params.cpp#L507-L510

Line 509 will be called at the same time, which will crash the program.

The above idea is also verified by Adress Sanitizer, which outputs the following info:

    ==28245==ERROR: AddressSanitizer: attempting double-free on 0x6060019bb5a0 in thread T3:

issue_title: File exit
LD_LIBRARY_PATH and DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH not imported on OS X
issue_body:
I tried to import GPU Tensorflow; it failed to find the CUDA libraries. I checked the `process.env` environment variable and found that `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` and `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH` had not been added. After further digging, I found that the `shell-env` package doesn't import them because it just calls the `env` command, which doesn't include them in its output for some reason.

Manually going to the console, adding the paths to `process.env`, and restarting the kernel fixed the problem.

I'm not sure what the solution is here. Either import these separately, allow them to be configured somewhere, or something else? I think there are enough people who want to call CUDA from Python that it's probably worth doing though.

issue_title: Subtitle support
issue_body:
It would be nice if mpsyt could support YouTube subtitles.

I've spent a few hours on it and found out that it is quite easy to fetch the subtitle list, the text and convert it to .srt format (which can be opened by players like mpv or mplayer), as shown in the following sketch of code I've just written:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

# used to fetch the content from a url
import urllib.request as ur

# used to parse the xml
import xml.etree.ElementTree as et

# used to unescape the text, e.g., < to >
import html

# used to get the temp dir
import tempfile

# dictionary which keeps the order of insertion
from collections import OrderedDict as od


# Receives a time in seconds
# Returns its representation in the srt format hh:mm:ss:MMM
def time_to_str(time):
    h = int(time/3600)
    m = int((time % 3600) / 60)
    s = int(time % 60)
    ms = int((1000 * time) % 1000)
    return '%02d:%02d:%02d,%03d' % (h, m, s, ms)

# Receives a list of tuples in the form (start, end, text)
# Returns a string that can be saved to a valid .srt file
def track_to_srt(texts):
    i = 1
    srt = ''
    events = {}
    for text in texts:
        start, end = text[0], text[0] + text[1]
        if start in events:
            start += 0.00001 # prevent conflict
        events[start] = html.unescape(text[2])
        events[end] = None
    # This function may seem too much complicated, but I think this is needed 
    #  to deal with live closed-captions with superposition.
    text = []
    i = 1
    start = 0
    for t in sorted(events):
        # This will deal with live closed-captions to prevent hiding an old line
        #  before showing a new one, if these events are too close.
        # This doesn't affect normal subtitles, because in those there is no
        #  superposition, therefore events[t] will always be None when text is non
        #  empty, and the following variable will always be true.
        update = not (text and events[t] is not None and t - start < 0.15)
        # if there is some text, put it into the srt
        if text and update:
            srt += str(i)
            srt += '\n' + time_to_str(start) + ' --> ' + time_to_str(t)
            srt += '\n' + '\n'.join(text) + '\n\n'
            i += 1
        # change
        if events[t] is None and text: # removing
            text.pop(0)
        else: # adding
            text.append(events[t])
        if update:
            start = t
    # If there were only normal subtitles, the following would be enough instead of
    #  this entire function:
    '''
    for text in texts:
        srt += str(i)
        srt += '\n' + time_to_str(text[0]) + ' --> ' + time_to_str(text[0] + text[1])
        srt += '\n' + html.unescape(text[2]) + '\n\n'
        i += 1
    '''
    return srt

def get_text_from_url(url):
    return ur.urlopen(url).read()



class SubtitleFetcher():
    def __init__(self, video_id):
        self.video_id = video_id
        self.tracks = od()
        self.targets = od()

    # Returns two dicts:
    #   tracks: a dict with the track ids as keys and tuples (name, lang_code, lang_name, can_be_translated) as values
    #   targets: a dict with the languages ids as keys and tuples (lang_code, lang_name) as values
    def get_track_list(self):
        # Fetch list of available tracks and languages
        t = get_text_from_url('http://www.youtube.com/api/timedtext?type=list&v=' + self.video_id + '&tlangs=1')
        # Parse the xml content
        # Tracks:
        root = et.fromstring(t)
        self.tracks.clear()
        for track in root.findall('track'):
            att = track.attrib
            try: # deal with it even if cantran is missing
                can_translate = (att['cantran'] == 'true')
            except KeyError:
                can_translate = False
            self.tracks[att['id']] = (att['name'], att['lang_code'], att['lang_translated'], can_translate)
        # Languages available to translate:
        self.targets.clear()
        for target in root.findall('target'):
            att = target.attrib
            self.targets[att['id']] = (att['lang_code'], att['lang_translated'])
        return self.tracks, self.targets

    # Given the ids of the track and the target language, returns a string
    #   ready to save as an .srt subtitle file
    # Must not be called before the get_track_list function.
    # If the last parameter is None, the original track is used.
    def get_track_srt(self, track_id, lang_id = None):
        try:
            name = self.tracks[track_id][0]
            lang = self.tracks[track_id][1]
            tlang = self.targets[lang_id][0] if lang_id else None
        except KeyError:
            raise ValueError
        end = '&tlang=' + tlang if tlang else ''
        # Fetch the text
        t = get_text_from_url('http://www.youtube.com/api/timedtext?type=track&v=%s&name=%s&lang=%s' % 
            (self.video_id, name, lang) + end)
        # Parse the xml content
        root = et.fromstring(t)
        texts = []
        for text in root.findall('text'):
            texts.append((float(text.attrib['start']), float(text.attrib['dur']), text.text))
        # Sort by the initial time (there's no guarantee that it comes ordered)
        texts.sort(key = lambda t : t[0])
        return track_to_srt(texts)
    
    def can_translate(self, track_id):
        try:
            return self.tracks[track_id][3]
        except KeyError:
            raise ValueError


# Checking that it works:

video_id = input('Type or paste the 11-character video_id: ').strip()
if len(video_id) != 11:
    print('Invalid video id.')
    exit(0)
sf = SubtitleFetcher(video_id)
tracks, targets = sf.get_track_list()

if len(tracks) == 0:
    print('There are no subtitles avaiable for this video.')
    exit(0)

print('%3s  %-25s  %s' % ('ID', 'Language', 'Name'))
print('-' * 80)
for track_id, track in tracks.items():
    print('%3s  %-25s  %s' % (track_id, track[2], track[0]))
print('-' * 80)
track_id = input('Choose one of the above ids: ').strip()

# if the track can be translated and the user wants it to be translated
if sf.can_translate(track_id) and input('Translate? [Y/N] ').strip().upper().startswith('Y'):
    print('%7s %s' % ('ID', 'Language'))
    print('-' * 80)
    for target_id, target in targets.items():
        print('%3s %s' % (target_id, target[1]))
    print('-' * 80)
    tlang_id = input('Choose one of the above ids: ').strip()
else:
    tlang_id = None

srt = sf.get_track_srt(track_id, tlang_id)
filename = tempfile.gettempdir() + '/' + video_id + '.srt'
try:
    with open(filename, 'w') as f:
        f.write(srt)
except IOError:
    print('Could not save file.')
    exit(1)
print('File saved as "%s".' % filename)

```

Note: This does not not work with auto-generated subtitles.

issue_title: High resolution 'true_color' composite issue for Himawari-8 AHI
issue_body:
#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

```python
from satpy.scene import Scene
from datetime import datetime, timedelta


ppp_config_directory = "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/satpy-0.7.5-py2.7.egg/satpy/etc/"
path = "/Users/andyprata/Documents/python/himawari-8/hsd"

time_slot_str = time_slot.strftime("%Y%m%d%H%M")
fn_out = 'global_hima_'+time_slot_str
print "Processing: "+time_slot_str
global_scene = Scene(platform_name="Himawari-8", sensor="ahi", reader="ahi_hsd",
                     start_time=time_slot,
                     end_time=time_slot + timedelta(minutes=10),
                     base_dir=path,
                     ppp_config_dir=ppp_config_directory)

rgb_composite = 'true_color'

issue_title: to_unicode() is harmful
issue_body:
I've had some version of this bug more than once:

```
>>> from hyperlink import URL
>>> URL.from_text(b"/foo")
URL.from_text("b'/foo'")
```

Note the `bytes` were interpreted as `"b'/foo'"` instead of the (almost certainly) more-expected `"/foo"`.

This is because `URL.from_text(s)` calls `to_unicode(s)` which calls `unicode(s)`.

This "works" in Python 2 and does the above in Python 3.

It seems to me that raising an exception insofar as `from_text` should be given text.

issue_title: Drop request from event
issue_body: This is a follow-up to #945. Right now events are gathered by resource/parent. When this happens, `payload` becomes essentially meaningless. At best we provide "one of" the payloads that makes sense given the `impacted_records`. The same is true for `request`. Why do we need to provide one of these for each event? Under what circumstances does `request` vary for different `impacted_records` across events in a `request`, or within an event group in the same resource/parent? If there are such circumstances, should we move `request` into each `impacted_record`? If not, can we stop storing `request` for each event, since we already have the request when we're processing its events? This is a sincere question -- my inclination is to deprecate `payload` and `request`, but I know there are a bunch of places where we fabricate requests for reasons and I haven't thought through the implications of what that means for events (e.g. https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/blob/master/kinto/plugins/default_bucket/__init__.py and https://github.com/Kinto/kinto-signer/blob/master/kinto_signer/utils.py#L171).

issue_title: wraparound in pywt.swt
issue_body:
When performing swt using a Haar wavelet, the last index in the L-filter is calculated as 1/sqrt(2) * (index[-1] + index[0]), i.e. it seems as if the function wraps around. Is this intentional? I'm certainly no expert on wavelet transforms. Here is the output from some simple tests I did in jupyter:

Test1, length = 8
In: [5 4 3 0 0 0 0 0]
Out-H: [ 6.364   4.9497  2.1213  0.      0.      0.      0.      3.5355]
Out-L: [ 0.7071  0.7071  2.1213  0.      0.      0.      0.     -3.5355]

Test2, length = 6
In: [5 4 3 0 0 0]
Out-H: [ 6.364   4.9497  2.1213  0.      0.      3.5355]
Out-L: [ 0.7071  0.7071  2.1213  0.      0.     -3.5355]

Test3, reverse
In: [0 0 0 0 0 3 4 5]
Out-H: [ 0.      0.      0.      0.      2.1213  4.9497  6.364   3.5355]
Out-L: [ 0.      0.      0.      0.     -2.1213 -0.7071 -0.7071  3.5355]

Test4, reverse with extra padding
In: [0 0 0 0 0 3 4 5 0 0]

Out-H: [ 6.364   2.1213  0.      0.    ]
Out-L: [ 0.7071  2.1213  0.      0.    ]

issue_title: Can't plot booleans
issue_body:
Small proof of concept in Python:

    from pyspark.sql.types import Row
    Item = Row('b', 'i')
    rdd = sc.parallelize([Item(True, 100), Item(False, 200)])
    df = sqlContext.createDataFrame(rdd)
    df.registerTempTable('df')
    
    %%sql
    SELECT * FROM df

Then choose Bar -> X = b, Y = i (a plot which should work), and no data shows up.  

I traced this to an issue with plotly.  Plotly doesn't get how to plot bools, apparently.  Proof of concept:

    %%local
    from plotly.graph_objs import Bar, Figure, Data
    from plotly.offline import iplot
    l = Bar(x=[True, False], y=[100,200])
    fig = Figure(data=Data([l]))
    iplot(fig)

How do we solve this?  Is it a matter of coercing boolean columns to strings before plotting?

issue_title: As a user, units which don't pass the signature check are not imported
issue_body:
1. create repo with feed and specify list of allowed keys( which are different from the signing keys of synced content).
2 . sync repo
3. check that none of those packages were imported into the repo
4. check the counter of not imported packages is correct

1. create repo with feed and specify list of allowed keys( which is the same as the signing keys of synced content).
2 . sync repo
3. check that packages were imported into the repo

1. create repo with feed and set require-signature to True. It is better to provide a feed where there are several types of packages like here https://repos.fedorapeople.org/repos/pulp/pulp/demo_repos/test_drpm_repo/ DRPMs + SRPMs
2 . sync repo
3. check that none of those packages were imported into the repo because they are unsigned
4. check the counter of not importer packages is correct ( sum of drpms and srpms)
5. update require-signature to False
6. sync repo again
7. check that all packages were imported into the repo

Since there are 2 config parameters --require-signature and --allowed-keys you can test different scenarios where you:
1) require packages to be signed and provide a specific list of keys
2) require packages just to be signed but you don't care with which keys( i.e allowed-keys is empty)
3) you allow unsigned packages but those which are signed should be signed with specific keys that are in the list of allowed-keys

Since signature policy is not compatible with lazy sync, confirm that:
1) during repo creation you cannot have on_demand, background policy and signature policy enabled
2) during repo update you cannot switch to on_deman, background policy if signature policy is enabled.
signature policy enabled means - you have some keys specified in the allowed-keys or require-signature is set to True.

Keys that are provided into allowed-keys should be 8 charters long. Confirm that providing keys that has different length will raise an error.

This functionally was implemented not just for sync but for copy and upload. so you will have here a bit of work..

Some copy scenarios:
1) create repoA with on_demand policy and sync it
2) create repoB with signature policy enabled
3) copy from repoA to repoB and confirm that no packages were imported into repoB

1) create repoA  and sync it( for example sync unsigned packages)
2) create repoB with signature policy enabled( require-signature set to True)
3) copy from repoA to repoB and confirm that no packages were imported into repoB

Upload is the most simple here.
1) create repo with signature policy enabled
2) upload a concrete file  or from a directory
3) confirm package was imported or not based on yours signature policy settings.

issue_title: Verifying Pulp 2.y compatability with Celery 3.1, Kombu 3.0 AND Celery 4.0, Kombu 4.0
issue_body:
This is a verification for [Issue 2527](https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2527). This change should allow pulp smash to show the same results in an environment like this which will come with F26:

```
amqp==2.1.4
billiard==3.5.0.2
celery==4.0.2
kombu==4.0.2+a patch to be released via RPM in Fedora
vine==1.1.3
```

Versus a typical environment today on other distros:


```
amqp==1.4.9
billiard==3.3.0.23
celery==3.1.20
kombu==3.0.33
```

To test this we use Pulp smash by running them on both environments.

Smash an F26 Environment
------------------------------------

1. On a fresh rpm installation in an F26 environment, ensure the following packages are installed via RPM and come from the Fedora base or updates repos: amqp, billiard, celery, kombu, and vine. This should deliver the "new" dependency stack.
2. Run pulp-smash and record any failures

Smash a F25 Environment
----------------------------------
1. This should have the old dependency stack with an rpm based install.
2. Run pulp-smash and record any failures.

issue_title: ClientIDMutation differs from mutationWithClientMutationId(javascript)?
Django debug toolbar conflicts
issue_body:
[This commit](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/commit/c5b15cec2f0a7082006b94314ca45ee1322af60d) attempted to fix the conflict between DDT and the SQL tracking in the debug middleware (formerly plugin).

Unfortunately this doesn't seem to work at the moment. We delete `connection.cursor` in the `unwrap_cursor` functions in both [Graphene](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/master/graphene/contrib/django/debug/sql/tracking.py#L51) and [DDT](https://github.com/django-debug-toolbar/django-debug-toolbar/blob/0ed1538374d3dc41d51fc562b4e8be6ce8208552/debug_toolbar/panels/sql/tracking.py#L51).

I'm not 100% sure what the correct approach here should be, especially within graphene as the DDT code will execute later. Would having both sets of tracking set up result in a problem anyway?

For the moment, you can disable the SQL panel in DDT when you want to use the middleware. Perhaps if we can't have both cursor wrappers enabled, we could detect the `hasattr(connection, '_djdt_cursor')` case and print a more helpful message.

issue_title: Passing the request_context to graphql
issue_body:
Not sure if this is intended or not but shouldn't the context be taken outside the message processing?
In base.py:

```py
def execute(self, request_context, params):
        return graphql(
            self.schema, **dict(params, allow_subscriptions=True))
```

Should be:

```py
self.schema, **dict(params, context_value= request_context, allow_subscriptions=True))
```

Otherwise, how can you pass the `request_context`? Does it make sense to be per message? Or is it intended for another use?

issue_title: Version 1.6.0 should probably be 2.0.0
issue_body: Just wanted to point that out, since Jazzband states about BREAKING.FEATURE.FIX release numbering (https://jazzband.co/docs/releases), django-pipeline 1.6 should probably be released as 2.0, because of the breaking change in how to configure it (i.e. `PIPELINE` dict setting). In other words, user can't just bump version number of django-pipeline (from 1.5.x to 1.6) without changing the code (configuration). Although I'm glad that this is documented (http://django-pipeline.readthedocs.org/en/latest/installation.html#upgrading-from-1-5), thanks.

issue_title: cell_value (matrix.py) function is slow in python2
issue_body:
Line 83 in matrix.py 
`if row in self.row_range() and column in self.column_range():`
is not optimal when using python2. Membership test in xrange is very slow when the member value increases. For example try running 
`100000000 in xrange(1000000000)` vs `1 in xrange(1000000000)`

It would be faster to use 
`if row < self.number_of_rows() and column < self.number_of_columns():`

issue_title: Enable crowdsourced video caption creation
issue_body:
Having captions for all the videos would improve both the searchability and the accessibility of the site.  Doing it all at once would be a huge project, but encouraging site users to submit captions could make it possible to gradually get them all done.  MIT has a [captioning resources page](https://ux.mit.edu/accessibility/captions) that looks pretty useful.  It mentions [Amara](http://www.amara.org/en/), which is in use on pyvideo (but seems to have some [issues](https://github.com/pyvideo/richard/issues/284), at least as currently implemented).

We should at least make sure we allow usage of whatever Amara and YouTube captions and/or PyCon US live captioning already exists.
